subject: ENT
question: A 5-year-old girl presents to you with intellectual disability and hearing loss and you notice the following external ear anomaly. What could be the anomaly in this case be?
options: Ganglion
Preauricular sinus
Preauricular tag
Neurodermatitis

explanation.short: C. Preauricular tag
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Preauricular Tag

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Preauricular tags are congenital external ear anomalies that may be isolated or syndromic (e.g., Goldenhar syndrome). Any newborn with external ear anomalies should undergo hearing screening and evaluation for associated syndromes, as these anomalies may indicate underlying structural or genetic conditions affecting craniofacial development.
  • ➤ Preauricular tags are congenital external ear anomalies that may be isolated or syndromic (e.g., Goldenhar syndrome). Any newborn with external ear anomalies should undergo hearing screening and evaluation for associated syndromes, as these anomalies may indicate underlying structural or genetic conditions affecting craniofacial development.

subject: ENT
question: A 22-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a painful, swollen left ear following a physical altercation. On examination, the left auricle is markedly swollen and deformed with a fluctuant, non-erythematous area occupying most of the antihelical fold. There is no laceration or discharge, and the patient denies any hearing loss. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of this condition?
options: Cold compress and analgesics
Incision and drainage followed by pressure dressing
Oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up
Immediate referral for plastic surgery consultation

explanation.short: B. Incision and drainage followed by pressure dressing
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Incision and Drainage Followed by Pressure Dressing

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Auricular hematomas should be managed promptly with incision and drainage followed by a pressure dressing to prevent cartilage necrosis and permanent deformity (cauliflower ear). Cold compresses, antibiotics, or plastic surgery consultation alone are not sufficient initial treatments.
  • ➤ Auricular hematomas should be managed promptly with incision and drainage followed by a pressure dressing to prevent cartilage necrosis and permanent deformity (cauliflower ear).

  • ➤ cartilage necrosis and permanent deformity (cauliflower ear).
  • ➤ Cold compresses, antibiotics, or plastic surgery consultation alone are not sufficient initial treatments.

subject: ENT
question: A 6-year-old child is scheduled for a pinnaplasty due to prominent ears. The parents inquire about the rationale for choosing this age for the procedure. Which of the following is the most appropriate reason for performing pinnaplasty at this age?
options: Ear cartilage has reached full adult size.
Chondral cartilage (graft) matures adequately
Prevention of psychological distress from peer teasing.
All of the above

explanation.short: D. All of the above
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) All of the Above

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Pinnaplasty (Otoplasty) is ideally performed at around 6 years of age because: The external ear has reached near-adult size (~85–90%) . The cartilage is mature yet flexible enough for reshaping . Psychological distress from peer teasing can be prevented . A combination of these factors makes 6 years the optimal time for the procedure.
  • ➤ Pinnaplasty (Otoplasty) is ideally performed at around 6 years of age because: The external ear has reached near-adult size (~85–90%) . The cartilage is mature yet flexible enough for reshaping . Psychological distress from peer teasing can be prevented . A combination of these factors makes 6 years the optimal time for the procedure.
  • ➤ Pinnaplasty (Otoplasty) is ideally performed at around 6 years of age
  • ➤ The external ear has reached near-adult size (~85–90%) . The cartilage is mature yet flexible enough for reshaping . Psychological distress from peer teasing can be prevented . A combination of these factors makes 6 years the optimal time for the procedure.

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with a three-day history of pain and swelling in his right ear. He has no significant past medical history but recalls scratching his ear with a toothpick a few days ago. On examination, the right pinna is erythematous, swollen, and tender to touch, especially around the cartilage. There is no discharge or fluctuance, and the auditory canal appears normal. The tympanic membrane is intact and mobile. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management of this condition?
options: Oral Antivirals and Analgesics
Oral Antibiotics and Analgesics
Incision and Drainage
Topical Antibiotic Ear Drops

explanation.short: B. Oral Antibiotics and Analgesics
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Oral Antibiotics and Analgesics

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Perichondritis is an infection of the auricular cartilage , commonly occurring after trauma and often caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa . Early recognition and treatment with systemic antibiotics (fluoroquinolones) are crucial to prevent cartilage necrosis and auricular deformity . Incision and drainage are reserved for abscess formation , and topical antibiotics are ineffective for deep cartilage infections .
  • ➤ Perichondritis is an infection of the auricular cartilage , commonly occurring after trauma and often caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa . Early recognition and treatment with systemic antibiotics (fluoroquinolones) are crucial to prevent cartilage necrosis and auricular deformity . Incision and drainage are reserved for abscess formation , and topical antibiotics are ineffective for deep cartilage infections .
  • ➤ Perichondritis

  • ➤ after trauma
  • ➤ Pseudomonas aeruginosa
  • ➤ Early recognition and treatment with systemic antibiotics (fluoroquinolones) are crucial
  • ➤ cartilage necrosis and auricular deformity
  • ➤ Incision and drainage are reserved for abscess formation
  • ➤ topical antibiotics are ineffective for deep cartilage infections

subject: ENT
question: A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a small pit near her right ear, which has been present since birth. On examination, a small opening is noted anterior to the ascending limb of the helix of the right ear. There is no redness, swelling, or drainage at the time of the visit. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of this condition?
options: Surgical Excision of the Sinus Tract
Topical Antibiotic Ointment Application
Systemic Antibiotics
Observation and Hygiene Education

explanation.short: D. Observation and Hygiene Education
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Observation and Hygiene Education

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ A preauricular sinus is a congenital defect resulting from incomplete fusion of the first and second pharyngeal arches . Asymptomatic cases require only observation and hygiene education . Surgical excision and antibiotics are reserved for infected or recurrently symptomatic cases.
  • ➤ A preauricular sinus is a congenital defect resulting from incomplete fusion of the first and second pharyngeal arches .

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old patient presents with recurrent episodes of temporomandibular joint (TMJ) pain and occasional otorrhea, especially after chewing. Examination reveals a small opening in the antero-inferior part of the bony ear canal. This anatomical variation is known as the foramen of Huschke. What is the most likely clinical significance of this finding?
options: Increased risk of Cholesteatoma formation
Potential for spread of infection from the TMJ to the ear
Abnormality leading to conductive hearing loss
Increased risk of otosclerosis

explanation.short: B. Potential for spread of infection from the TMJ to the ear
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Potential for spread of infection from the TMJ to the Ear

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The foramen of Huschke is a congenital defect in the antero-inferior bony external auditory canal that creates a communication between the TMJ and the ear . This can lead to the spread of TMJ infections to the ear , causing otorrhea, ear pain, and symptoms that worsen with chewing .
  • ➤ The foramen of Huschke is a congenital defect in the antero-inferior bony external auditory canal that creates a communication between the TMJ and the ear .
  • ➤ foramen of Huschke
  • ➤ congenital defect in the antero-inferior bony external auditory canal
  • ➤ communication between the TMJ and the ear
  • ➤ This can lead to the spread of TMJ infections to the ear , causing otorrhea, ear pain, and symptoms that worsen with chewing .
  • ➤ can lead to the spread of TMJ infections to the ear
  • ➤ otorrhea, ear pain, and symptoms that worsen with chewing
  • ➤ Ref. P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 2.

subject: ENT
question: A 45-year-old male presents with a laceration on the upper part of his left ear following a minor car accident. He reports numbness in the area surrounding the laceration. On examination, the laceration involves the helix but does not extend to the external auditory canal. Which nerve is most likely affected in this patient's injury?
options: Greater Auricular Nerve
Lesser Occipital Nerve
Auriculotemporal Nerve
Arnold's Nerve (a branch of the Vagus Nerve)

explanation.short: A. Greater Auricular Nerve
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Greater Auricular Nerve

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The Greater Auricular Nerve (C2, C3) is the primary sensory nerve for most of the external ear, including the helix and lobule . Injury to this nerve during trauma can cause numbness in these regions . The auriculotemporal nerve, lesser occipital nerve, and Arnold’s nerve provide sensation to other parts of the ear but do not primarily supply the helix.
  • ➤ The Greater Auricular Nerve (C2, C3) is the primary sensory nerve for most of the external ear, including the helix and lobule .
  • ➤ Greater Auricular Nerve (C2, C3) is the primary sensory nerve for most of the external ear, including the helix and lobule
  • ➤ Injury to this nerve during trauma can cause numbness in these regions .
  • ➤ Injury to this nerve during trauma can cause numbness in these regions
  • ➤ The auriculotemporal nerve, lesser occipital nerve, and Arnold’s nerve provide sensation to other parts of the ear but do not primarily supply the helix.
  • ➤ The auriculotemporal nerve, lesser occipital nerve, and Arnold’s nerve provide sensation to other parts of the ear but do not primarily supply the helix.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 4.

subject: ENT
question: A 4-year-old child is brought to the pediatrician by his parents for a routine check-up. The parents express concern about the shape of the child's ears, which they describe as small and underdeveloped. On examination, the child's ears are noted to be smaller than normal with poorly formed helices and lobules, resembling a peanut shape. The child has normal hearing. Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with this ear deformity?
options: Turner Syndrome
Treacher Collins Syndrome
Down Syndrome
Microtia

explanation.short: D. Microtia
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Microtia

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The "peanut ear" deformity, characterized by small, underdeveloped ears with poorly formed helices and lobules, is most commonly associated with microtia. Microtia is a congenital external ear malformation that can range in severity and may be associated with other craniofacial anomalies or occur in isolation.
  • ➤ The "peanut ear" deformity, characterized by small, underdeveloped ears with poorly formed helices and lobules, is most commonly associated with microtia.
  • ➤ Microtia is a congenital external ear malformation that can range in severity and may be associated with other craniofacial anomalies or occur in isolation.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 48.

subject: ENT
question: A 20-year-old female presents to the clinic with pain and swelling in front of her left ear. She has a history of a small pit in that area since birth but has never had any problems until now. On examination, there is erythema, tenderness, and swelling anterior to the tragus of the left ear, and a small amount of purulent discharge is noted from a pit in the same region. There is no fever or hearing loss. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this condition?
options: Incision and drainage of the sinus
Systemic antibiotics targeting skin flora
Topical antibiotic ointment application
Surgical excision of the sinus tract

explanation.short: B. Systemic antibiotics targeting skin flora
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Systemic Antibiotics Targeting Skin Flora

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The appropriate initial management for an infected preauricular sinus is systemic antibiotics targeting common skin flora (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus spp.) . Surgical excision is reserved for recurrent or complicated cases . Incision and drainage are needed only in cases of abscess formation .
  • ➤ The appropriate initial management for an infected preauricular sinus is systemic antibiotics targeting common skin flora (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus spp.) . Surgical excision is reserved for recurrent or complicated cases . Incision and drainage are needed only in cases of abscess formation .
  • ➤ appropriate initial management for an infected preauricular sinus is systemic antibiotics targeting common skin flora (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus spp.)
  • ➤ Surgical excision is reserved for recurrent or complicated cases
  • ➤ Incision and drainage are needed only in cases of abscess formation

subject: ENT
question: A 24-year-old female presents to the clinic complaining of a progressively enlarging, raised scar on her left earlobe following a piercing six months ago. On examination, the lesion is firm, rubbery, and larger than the original wound, extending beyond the borders of the initial piercing site. The patient reports that the lesion is occasionally itchy and painful. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this condition?
options: Topical Antibiotics
Intralesional Corticosteroid Injections
Surgical Excision
Application of Silicone Gel Sheets

explanation.short: B. Intralesional Corticosteroid Injections
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Intralesional Corticosteroid Injections

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Keloids are hypertrophic scars that extend beyond the original wound margins due to excessive collagen deposition. First-line treatment includes intralesional corticosteroid injections, which help reduce size, inflammation, and pruritus. Surgical excision has a high recurrence rate unless combined with adjunctive therapy.
  • ➤ Keloids are hypertrophic scars that extend beyond the original wound margins due to excessive collagen deposition.
  • ➤ Keloids are hypertrophic scars that extend beyond the original wound margins due to excessive collagen deposition.
  • ➤ First-line treatment includes intralesional corticosteroid injections, which help reduce size, inflammation, and pruritus.
  • ➤ First-line treatment includes intralesional corticosteroid injections, which help reduce size, inflammation, and pruritus.

  • ➤ Surgical excision has a high recurrence rate unless combined with adjunctive therapy.

subject: ENT
question: A 35-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a painful, swollen left ear. He has a history of ear piercing two weeks ago. On examination, the upper part of the ear is erythematous, tender, and edematous, with sparing of the earlobe. The external auditory canal and tympanic membrane are normal. Which microorganism is most commonly responsible for this patient’s condition?
options: Staphylococcus Aureus
Pseudomonas Aeruginosa
Aspergillus Niger
Streptococcus Pyogenes

explanation.short: B. Pseudomonas Aeruginosa
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Pseudomonas Aeruginosa

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Perichondritis is an infection of the auricular cartilage, most commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, particularly following ear piercing or trauma. The earlobe is typically spared, distinguishing it from cellulitis. Prompt antibiotic treatment with fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) is essential to prevent cartilage necrosis and ear deformity.
  • ➤ Perichondritis is an infection of the auricular cartilage, most commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, particularly following ear piercing or trauma.
  • ➤ The earlobe is typically spared, distinguishing it from cellulitis. Prompt antibiotic treatment with fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) is essential to prevent cartilage necrosis and ear deformity.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 50.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 50.

subject: ENT
question: A 12-year-old patient presents with recurrent infections characterized by drainage from an opening in the neck just below and behind the angle of the mandible and another opening in the external auditory canal. These findings are consistent with a collaural fistula, an abnormality of the first branchial cleft. What is the most appropriate initial management for this condition?
options: Antibiotic therapy and observation
Surgical excision of the fistula
Corticosteroid therapy
Hearing assessment and rehabilitation

explanation.short: B. Surgical excision of the fistula
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Surgical Excision of the Fistula

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ A collaural fistula , a first branchial cleft anomaly , requires surgical excision to prevent recurrent infections and complications . Antibiotics may be used to treat acute infections , but they do not provide a definitive cure. The fistula runs near the facial nerve, requiring careful surgical dissection to avoid nerve damage .
  • ➤ A collaural fistula , a first branchial cleft anomaly , requires surgical excision to prevent recurrent infections and complications .

subject: ENT
question: During embryonic development, the pinna (auricle) forms from contributions of specific embryonic structures. Which of the following correctly identifies the embryonic origin of the pinna?
options: Neural crest cells from the first pharyngeal arch
Mesoderm of the second pharyngeal arch
Ectodermal hillocks of the first and second pharyngeal arches
Endodermal contributions from the first pharyngeal pouch

explanation.short: C. Ectodermal hillocks of the first and second pharyngeal arches
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Ectodermal Hillocks of the First and Second Pharyngeal Arches

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The pinna (auricle) develops from six ectodermal hillocks located around the first and second pharyngeal arches , which fuse and remodel to form the external ear . The first pharyngeal arch contributes to the tragus and upper helix, while the second pharyngeal arch forms the remaining auricular structures .
  • ➤ The pinna (auricle) develops from six ectodermal hillocks located around the first and second pharyngeal arches , which fuse and remodel to form the external ear .
  • ➤ pinna (auricle) develops from six ectodermal hillocks
  • ➤ first and second pharyngeal arches
  • ➤ fuse and remodel
  • ➤ external ear
  • ➤ The first pharyngeal arch contributes to the tragus and upper helix, while the second pharyngeal arch forms the remaining auricular structures .
  • ➤ The first pharyngeal arch contributes to the tragus and upper helix, while the second pharyngeal arch forms the remaining auricular structures

subject: ENT
question: A wrestler presents to your OPD with the following ear deformity. What is the most likely cause for this?
options: Auricular cyst
Avulsion of pinna
External ear exostosis
Auricular hematoma

explanation.short: D. Auricular hematoma
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Auricular Hematoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Auricular hematoma occurs due to blunt trauma, leading to blood accumulation between cartilage and perichondrium. If untreated, it progresses to cauliflower ear, a permanent deformity. Immediate aspiration, pressure dressing, and prophylactic antibiotics are key to preventing complications.
  • ➤ Auricular hematoma occurs due to blunt trauma, leading to blood accumulation between cartilage and perichondrium.
  • ➤ If untreated, it progresses to cauliflower ear, a permanent deformity. Immediate aspiration, pressure dressing, and prophylactic antibiotics are key to preventing complications.

subject: ENT
question: A newborn present with the following condition bilaterally and with external auditory canal atresia. Corrective surgery is usually performed at
options: &lt; 1 year of age
6 years of age
Puberty
Adulthood

explanation.short: B. 6 years of age
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) 6 years of age

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Microtia is a congenital ear anomaly that requires auricular reconstruction, optimally performed between 6 years of age when the ear has grown sufficiently, and rib cartilage is available. External auditory canal atresia often coexists, requiring bone-anchored hearing aids (BAHA) or canaloplasty for hearing rehabilitation.
  • ➤ Microtia is a congenital ear anomaly that requires auricular reconstruction, optimally performed between 6 years of age when the ear has grown sufficiently, and rib cartilage is available.

Application of 0.5% silver nitrate soaks for superficial infection,
Protection of bullae from rupture

Cellulitis

subject: ENT
question: A 32-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a deep laceration of the left pinna, sustained in a road traffic accident. Examination reveals a complex laceration involving the skin, perichondrium, and cartilage, as seen in the image. What is the best immediate management approach?
options: Primary closure with absorbable sutures for perichondrium and nonabsorbable sutures for skin
Immediate debridement with cartilage excision and skin grafting
Leave the wound open to heal by secondary intention
Apply a simple pressure dressing and follow up in one week

explanation.short: A. Primary closure with absorbable sutures for perichondrium and nonabsorbable sutures for skin
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Primary closure with absorbable sutures for perichondrium and nonabsorbable sutures for skin

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Ear pinna lacerations require early surgical repair to preserve cartilage viability and prevent deformities. The perichondrium must be sutured with absorbable sutures, and the skin should be closed with nonabsorbable sutures. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are recommended to prevent infection.

subject: ENT
question: A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department after a road traffic accident, where his external ear was partially torn but still attached by a small pedicle of skin. What is the best management approach?
options: Primary reattachment of the pinna
Immediate removal of the avulsed segment and skin grafting
Leaving the wound open to heal by secondary intention
Suturing the skin without addressing the cartilage

explanation.short: A. Primary reattachment of the pinna
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Primary reattachment of the pinna

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Pinna avulsion injuries require immediate surgical attention. Primary reattachment should always be attempted if the pinna is partially attached, while microvascular reimplantation may be attempted for complete avulsions. In cases where reimplantation is not possible, cartilage can be implanted under postauricular skin for later reconstruction.
  • ➤ Pinna avulsion injuries require immediate surgical attention. Primary reattachment should always be attempted if the pinna is partially attached, while microvascular reimplantation may be attempted for complete avulsions. In cases where reimplantation is not possible, cartilage can be implanted under postauricular skin for later reconstruction.

subject: ENT
question: A 72-year-old diabetic male presents to the emergency department with severe right ear pain and persistent otorrhea for the past two weeks. He reports that the pain is worse at night and does not improve with over-the-counter analgesics. Examination reveals tenderness over the external auditory canal (EAC), granulation tissue at the cartilaginous-bony junction, and cranial nerve VII weakness (right-sided facial palsy). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start intravenous ciprofloxacin and obtain imaging
Prescribe oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and schedule follow-up
Perform immediate surgical debridement of the external auditory canal
Start topical antibiotic ear drops and oral corticosteroids

explanation.short: A. Start intravenous ciprofloxacin and obtain imaging
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Start Intravenous Ciprofloxacin and Obtain Imaging

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa is a life-threatening Pseudomonas infection seen in elderly diabetics, characterized by severe otalgia, persistent otorrhea, granulation tissue in the external auditory canal, and possible cranial nerve palsies. Management includes IV ciprofloxacin, diabetes control, and prolonged antibiotic therapy (6–8 weeks).

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 52

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old male with chronic otitis externa presents to the clinic complaining of persistent ear discharge and hearing loss. He has a history of frequent cotton swab use for ear cleaning. Examination reveals copious purulent discharge in the external auditory canal, and the tympanic membrane is not visible. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in the management of this patient?
options: Prescribe Oral Antibiotics
Perform aural toilet
Immediate referral for surgical intervention
Initiate topical antifungal therapy

explanation.short: B. Perform aural toilet
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Perform Aural Toilet

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Aural toilet is the essential first step in managing chronic otitis externa with significant discharge. It allows for proper examination, diagnosis, and effective delivery of topical treatments. Oral antibiotics can be given next and surgery is reserved for complicated or refractory cases.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 49.

subject: ENT
question: A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a one-week history of itching and fullness in her right ear. She reports no pain but mentions a slight hearing reduction. She has a history of swimming regularly. On examination, the external auditory canal is erythematous and has black-headed filamentous debris. The tympanic membrane appears normal. Which microorganism is most commonly responsible for this patient’s condition?
options: Aspergillus Niger
Pseudomonas Aeruginosa
Candida Albicans
Staphylococcus Aureus

explanation.short: A. Aspergillus Niger
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Aspergillus Niger

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Aspergillus niger is the most common cause of otomycosis, a fungal infection of the external auditory canal. It presents with itching, ear fullness, and black-headed filamentous fungal debris, particularly in individuals with frequent swimming exposure. Management includes aural toilet and topical antifungal therapy.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 51.

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old male presents with acute onset of right-sided facial paralysis, severe ear pain, and a vesicular rash in his right ear. He also reports some hearing loss and vertigo on the same side. His past medical history is significant for chickenpox in childhood. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bell's Palsy
Acute Otitis Media
Ramsay Hunt Syndrome
Acoustic Neuroma

explanation.short: C. Ramsay Hunt Syndrome
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Ramsay Hunt Syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Ramsay Hunt Syndrome (Herpes Zoster Oticus) is caused by reactivation of Varicella-Zoster Virus in the geniculate ganglion, leading to a triad of ipsilateral facial paralysis, ear pain, and a vesicular rash in the ear or oral mucosa. It can also involve the vestibulocochlear nerve, causing hearing loss and vertigo. Early treatment with antivirals and corticosteroids improves outcomes.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 52.

subject: ENT
question: A 28-year-old patient presents to the clinic with impacted cerumen. During the consultation, the resident explains about the glands in the external auditory canal (EAC) that contribute to cerumen production. Which type of glands predominantly contribute to the production of cerumen in the EAC?
options: Eccrine Sweat Glands
Sebaceous Glands
Apocrine Sweat Glands
Salivary Glands

explanation.short: C. Apocrine Sweat Glands
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Apocrine Sweat Glands

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Cerumen (earwax) is primarily produced by apocrine sweat glands in the external auditory canal, with a minor contribution from sebaceous glands. It plays a protective role by preventing infections, trapping foreign particles, and lubricating the ear canal.

subject: ENT
question: During an otoscopic examination, a physician prepares to examine a 30-year-old adult patient's ear. What is the appropriate direction to pull the pinna for optimal visualization of the external auditory canal and tympanic membrane?
options: Straight forward
Downward and forward
Upward and backward
Upward and forward

explanation.short: C. Upward and backward
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Upward and Backward

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ For an otoscopic examination in adults, the pinna should be pulled upward and backward to straighten the external auditory canal, ensuring optimal visualization of the tympanic membrane. In children under 3 years, the pinna should be pulled downward and forward due to anatomical differences in the ear canal.

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old man presents with ear pain and decreased hearing in his left ear. On clinical examination, the physician intends to thoroughly evaluate the external auditory canal (EAC). Which of the following findings would be most concerning when examining the EAC?
options: Presence of cerumen
Bony protrusions along the canal wall
Granulation tissue at the floor of the canal
Slight erythema of the canal skin

explanation.short: C. Granulation tissue at the floor of the canal
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Granulation Tissue at the Floor of the Canal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Granulation tissue in the external auditory canal is a red flag that may indicate chronic infection (malignant otitis externa), cholesteatoma, or malignancy. Further evaluation with otoscopy, imaging, and biopsy is warranted in suspicious cases.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 2.

subject: ENT
question: A medical student is learning about ear anatomy during an otolaryngology rotation. The attending physician points out a narrow region in the external auditory canal (EAC) and explains that this area can sometimes obstruct the view of the tympanic membrane during an otoscopic examination. Which part of the EAC is being described, and what is its significance?
options: The lateral third, consisting of cartilage, allows for flexibility during examination.
The isthmus, the narrowest part of the EAC, can limit visualization of the tympanic membrane.
The medial two-thirds, made of bone, provide protection to deeper structures.
The junction where the bony and cartilaginous parts meet, often the site of cerumen impaction.

explanation.short: B. The isthmus, the narrowest part of the EAC, can limit visualization of the tympanic membrane.
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) The isthmus, the narrowest part of the EAC, can limit visualization of the tympanic membrane.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The isthmus is the narrowest part of the external auditory canal and can limit tympanic membrane visualization during otoscopy.

question: A 40-year-old patient presents with recurrent infections involving both the parotid gland and the external auditory canal. Which anatomical feature is most likely facilitating the spread of infection between these two regions?

Fissures of Santorini
Mastoid Air Cells involvement
Tympanic Membrane Perforation

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Fissures of Santorini

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

subject: ENT
question: A medical resident is learning about the anatomy of the external auditory canal (EAC) during an otolaryngology rotation. The attending physician explains the division of the EAC and its clinical importance. Which of the following correctly describes the division of the external auditory canal?
options: The outer one-third is bony and lined with hair follicles and ceruminous glands, while the inner two-thirds is cartilaginous and lacks these structures.
The entire EAC is cartilaginous and lined with skin containing hair follicles and ceruminous glands.
The outer one-third is cartilaginous and lined with hair follicles and ceruminous glands, while the inner two-thirds is bony and lacks these structures.
The EAC is divided into equal bony and cartilaginous halves, both lined with hair follicles and ceruminous glands.

explanation.short: C. The outer one-third is cartilaginous and lined with hair follicles and ceruminous glands, while the inner two-thirds is bony and lacks these structures.
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) The outer one-third is cartilaginous and lined with hair follicles and ceruminous glands, while the inner two-thirds is bony and lacks these structures.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The external auditory canal is divided into an outer cartilaginous third, which contains hair follicles and ceruminous glands, and an inner bony two-thirds, which lacks these structures. This anatomical knowledge is crucial for understanding ear pathology and clinical management.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 2.

subject: ENT
question: A 32-year-old female presents with acute onset of severe pain in her left ear. She denies any history of trauma or infection. On examination, she experiences intense pain when the anterior wall of the external auditory canal (EAC) is gently palpated. Which nerve is most likely responsible for the innervation of this area of the EAC?
options: The Auriculo-Temporal Nerve
The Greater Auricular Nerve
The Vagus Nerve
The Facial Nerve

explanation.short: A. The Auriculo-Temporal Nerve
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) The Auriculo-Temporal Nerve

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The auriculotemporal nerve, a branch of the mandibular nerve (V3) of the trigeminal nerve, provides sensory innervation to the anterior wall of the external auditory canal (EAC). Understanding the nerve supply is essential for diagnosing and managing ear pain.

subject: ENT
question: A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with a two-day history of severe pain in her right ear. She reports a sensation of fullness and decreased hearing on the affected side. On examination, the External Auditory Canal (EAC) is noted to be swollen with a visible red, tender nodule near the outer third. There is no discharge or tympanic membrane perforation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute otitis externa
Eustachian tube dysfunction
Furuncle of the external auditory canal
Cholesteatoma

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Furuncle of the External Auditory Canal

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

The most common causative organism is Staphylococcus aureus, and the tragal sign is positive.

question: A 33-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of right ear pain, itching, and hearing loss. He reports swimming in a lake over the weekend. Topical antibiotic drops with a corticosteroid

question: A 65-year-old diabetic patient presents with severe ear pain, otorrhea, and granulation tissue in the external auditory canal. The patient is diagnosed with malignant otitis externa. Which organism is most commonly responsible for this condition?

Staphylococcus aureus

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

subject: ENT
question: A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic with severe ear pain and swelling in his right ear for the past 3 days. He also reports tenderness while chewing and touching the external ear. On examination, there is a localized, erythematous swelling in the outer third of the external auditory canal. There is no purulent discharge, and the tympanic membrane is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute otitis externa (diffuse type)
Furuncle of the external auditory canal
Cholesteatoma
Acute otitis media

explanation.short: B. Furuncle of the external auditory canal
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Furuncle of the External Auditory Canal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Furuncles (localized staphylococcal infections) occur in the outer one-third of the external auditory canal because hair follicles are only present in this region. They present with severe localized pain and tenderness, and management includes warm compresses, antibiotics, and incision and drainage if necessary.

subject: ENT
question: A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with persistent cough and irritation whenever he cleans his ears with a cotton swab. He denies any fever, respiratory symptoms, or recent infections. Otoscopic examination reveals a normal external auditory canal and tympanic membrane. Which of the following nerves is responsible for this reflex?
options: Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)
Auriculotemporal nerve (Branch of CN V3)
Auricular branch of the vagus nerve (Arnold’s nerve, CN X)
Facial nerve (CN VII)

explanation.short: C. Auricular branch of the vagus nerve (Arnold’s nerve, CN X)
explanation.detail:
Ans. C. Auricular Branch of the Vagus Nerve (Arnold’s Nerve, CN X)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The auricular branch of the vagus nerve (Arnold’s nerve, CN X) innervates the posterior wall and floor of the external auditory canal. Stimulation of this nerve can trigger the Arnold’s Reflex (ear-cough reflex) due to vagal involvement in the respiratory tract.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 2

subject: ENT
question: A newborn is brought to the clinic for evaluation of right-sided congenital hearing loss. On examination, there is absence of the external auditory canal on the right side, while the auricle is normally developed. Which of the following best explains the embryological basis of this condition?
options: Failure of the first branchial arch to form properly
Incomplete recanalization of the meatal plug
Failure of the second branchial cleft to regress
Improper fusion of the hillocks of His

explanation.short: B. Incomplete recanalization of the meatal plug
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Incomplete Recanalization of the Meatal Plug

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The external auditory canal develops from the first branchial cleft. It initially forms as a meatal plug, which later recanalizes to form the bony canal lining. Failure of this recanalization results in congenital aural atresia, where the deeper canal may develop, but the outer part remains atretic.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 12

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 12

subject: ENT
question: A 12-year-old boy presents to the ENT clinic with recurrent swelling and discharge from a small opening just below and behind the angle of the mandible. His parents report that the swelling and discharge worsen during upper respiratory infections. On examination, there is a small pit in the external auditory canal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Empirical antibiotic therapy and observation
Surgical excision of the fistula
Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of the swelling
Incision and drainage of the fistula tract

explanation.short: B. Surgical excision of the fistula
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Surgical Excision of the Fistula

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ A collaural fistula is an abnormal first branchial cleft anomaly that presents as a persistent tract between the external auditory canal and the upper neck (below and behind the angle of the mandible). It requires surgical excision to prevent recurrent infections, with caution due to its proximity to the facial nerve.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 50,51

subject: ENT
question: A 32-year-old male presents with severe right ear pain and tenderness for the past 3 days. He reports that the pain worsens with jaw movements (chewing) and when touching his ear. Examination reveals a small, erythematous, tender swelling in the outer third of the external auditory canal. There is no otorrhea, and the tympanic membrane appears normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute Otitis Media
Acute Diffuse Otitis Externa
Furuncle of the External Auditory Canal
Cholesteatoma

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Furuncle of the External Auditory Canal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Furuncles (localized acute otitis externa) occur due to Staphylococcus aureus infection of hair follicles in the outer third of the external auditory canal. They present with severe pain, tenderness, and localized swelling. Management includes systemic antibiotics, analgesics, and warm compresses, with incision and drainage if an abscess forms.

subject: ENT
question: A 25-year-old male presents to the clinic with severe right ear pain, itching, and a sensation of fullness in the ear. He recently returned from a vacation where he went swimming daily. He denies fever or upper respiratory symptoms. Examination reveals an erythematous, swollen external auditory canal with purulent discharge. The tympanic membrane is not clearly visible due to canal swelling, and manipulation of the pinna elicits pain. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Prescribe oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
Perform ear toilet and insert a medicated wick
Prescribe oral antifungals
Immediate surgical drainage of the external auditory canal

explanation.short: B. Perform ear toilet and insert a medicated wick
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Perform Ear Toilet and Insert a Medicated Wick

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Diffuse otitis externa (swimmer’s ear) is a bacterial infection of the external auditory canal, commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus. The first step in management is ear toilet (cleaning of the canal), followed by insertion of a medicated wick with antibiotic-steroid drops. Systemic antibiotics are only needed for cellulitis or systemic symptoms.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 51,52

subject: ENT
question: A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with intense ear itching, a feeling of fullness, and mild discomfort in her right ear. She also reports watery discharge with a musty odor. She denies fever, hearing loss, or recent illness but mentions using topical antibiotic ear drops for a prior ear infection. Otoscopic examination reveals a black-headed filamentous growth in the external auditory canal. The tympanic membrane is normal. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
options: Staphylococcus aureus
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Aspergillus niger
Streptococcus pyogenes

explanation.short: C. Aspergillus niger
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Aspergillus niger

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Otomycosis (fungal otitis externa) is caused by Aspergillus niger (black-headed filamentous growth) or Candida albicans (white, creamy debris). It presents with intense itching, watery discharge with a musty odor, and ear fullness. Treatment involves thorough ear toilet and topical antifungal therapy (clotrimazole, nystatin, or salicylic acid in alcohol).

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 52.

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old female presents to the clinic with intense ear itching, irritation, and mild discomfort in her right ear for the past one week. She denies fever or pain but mentions that she recently used topical neomycin ear drops for a prior ear infection. Examination reveals erythematous, swollen external auditory canal skin with vesicles, oozing, and crusting. There is no purulent discharge. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue topical neomycin drops and observe
Prescribe oral antibiotics
Discontinue neomycin drops and apply a topical steroid cream
Perform surgical debridement of the ear canal

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Discontinue Neomycin Drops and Apply a Topical Steroid Cream

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Eczematous otitis externa is a hypersensitivity reaction, often triggered by topical antibiotics like neomycin. It presents with intense itching, erythema, vesicle formation, oozing, and crusting. Management includes discontinuation of the offending agent and application of topical steroid cream to reduce inflammation.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 52

question: A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic with intense itching of her right ear for the past several weeks. She reports that she frequently scratches her ear to relieve the itching. Recommend sympathetic psychotherapy and apply an ear pack

subject: ENT
question: A 50-year-old male presents with persistent right ear pain and purulent otorrhea for the past several months. He denies hearing loss or vertigo. Otoscopic examination reveals granulation tissue and exposed bone in the external auditory canal (EAC), with a normal tympanic membrane. There is no history of recent infection, but he underwent ear surgery several years ago. A biopsy is planned to rule out malignancy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Malignant otitis externa
Primary cholesteatoma of the external auditory canal
Chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM) with cholesteatoma
External ear carcinoma

explanation.short: B. Primary cholesteatoma of the external auditory canal
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Primary Cholesteatoma of the External Auditory Canal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Primary cholesteatoma of the external auditory canal is a rare condition where squamous epithelium invades the bony external canal, often after trauma or surgery. It presents with persistent otorrhea, pain, and granulation tissue, with a normal tympanic membrane. Management involves surgical removal of necrotic bone and histopathological evaluation to rule out malignancy.

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old male presents to the ENT clinic with progressive right ear fullness and mild conductive hearing loss. He denies pain, discharge, or recurrent infections. Otoscopic examination reveals a single, smooth, pedunculated, bony-hard mass arising from the posterior wall of the external auditory canal (EAC) near its outer end. The tympanic membrane is intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Exostosis
Osteoma of the External Auditory Canal
Cholesteatoma
Squamous cell carcinoma of the external ear

explanation.short: B. Osteoma of the External Auditory Canal
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Osteoma of the External Auditory Canal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Osteomas of the external auditory canal are benign, pedunculated bony tumors arising from cancellous bone, typically presenting as single, smooth masses on the posterior wall of the osseous canal near its outer end. They are usually asymptomatic but may require surgical removal if they cause conductive hearing loss or canal obstruction.

References:

subject: ENT
question: A 45-year-old male presents to the ENT clinic with gradual hearing loss and occasional ear blockage. He has a history of frequent cold-water exposure due to surfing. Examination reveals multiple smooth, sessile bony swellings in the deeper part of the external auditory canal near the tympanic membrane. The tympanic membrane is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Osteoma of the External Auditory Canal
Exostoses ("Surfer’s Ear")
Cholesteatoma
Malignant Otitis Externa

explanation.short: B. Exostoses ("Surfer’s Ear")
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Exostoses ("Surfer’s Ear")

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Exostoses ("Surfer’s Ear") are multiple, bilateral, sessile bony growths in the deep external auditory canal, commonly seen in individuals with repeated cold-water exposure. Management is conservative if asymptomatic; surgical removal is indicated if they cause hearing loss or recurrent wax impaction.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 107

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 107

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old male presents with gradual hearing loss and a sensation of ear fullness in his right ear. He denies pain, discharge, or a history of infections. Otoscopic examination reveals a smooth, firm, skin-covered polypoid swelling in the outer part of the external auditory canal (EAC), attached to the posterior wall. The lesion is non-tender, and there is accumulation of cerumen and debris due to obstruction. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Exostosis
Ceruminoma
Cholesteatoma
Squamous cell carcinoma of the external ear

explanation.short: B. Ceruminoma
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Ceruminoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Ceruminomas are tumors of ceruminous glands in the external auditory canal, presenting as smooth, firm, skin-covered polypoid swellings that can cause obstruction and cerumen retention. Management includes wide surgical excision, histological evaluation, and postoperative radiotherapy if malignancy is suspected.

References:

  • ↳ Reference: P.L. Dhingra - Diseases of Ear, Nose, and Throat & Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, Page 107

subject: ENT
question: A 60-year-old male presents with a painless, slow-growing mass in his right external auditory canal (EAC). He denies hearing loss, ear discharge, or recent infections. Otoscopic examination reveals a smooth, skin-covered swelling in the outer part of the meatus. The lesion is non-tender and does not involve the tympanic membrane. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Exostosis
Sebaceous Adenoma
Ceruminoma
Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the External Ear

explanation.short: B. Sebaceous Adenoma
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Sebaceous Adenoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Sebaceous adenomas are benign tumors of sebaceous glands in the external auditory canal, presenting as smooth, skin-covered swellings in the outer meatus. The treatment of choice is surgical excision, followed by histopathological evaluation to confirm diagnosis and rule out malignancy.

subject: ENT
question: A 65-year-old male with a history of chronic ear discharge presents with blood-stained otorrhea and severe ear pain for the past few weeks. He has also noticed progressive facial weakness on the affected side. Otoscopic examination reveals an ulcerated lesion with granulation tissue and a friable, bleeding polypoid mass in the external auditory canal. Regional lymph nodes are enlarged. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Chronic suppurative otitis media with granulation tissue
Malignant otitis externa
Squamous cell carcinoma of the external auditory canal
Ceruminoma

explanation.short: C. Squamous cell carcinoma of the external auditory canal
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the External Auditory Canal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the external auditory canal presents with chronic ear discharge that becomes blood-stained, severe otalgia, an ulcerated or polypoid bleeding mass, and possible facial nerve paralysis. Management involves en bloc surgical excision followed by postoperative radiotherapy.

References:

subject: ENT
question: A 70-year-old male presents with a painless ulcerated lesion on the posterior wall of his right external auditory canal. Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) of the external auditory canal
Malignant otitis externa

subject: ENT
question: A 45yr old female presented with gradual onset progressive hearing loss in the right ear associated with pulsatile tinnitus. The tympanic membrane appeared reddish. TFT showed Rinne was negative on the right side, positive on the left and Weber lateralised to the right. Tinnitus temporarily ceased with carotid pressure and auscultation over mastoid revealed an audible bruit. Most likely diagnosis:
options: ASOM
Glomus Jugulare
Acoustic Neuroma
Otosclerosis

explanation.short: B. Glomus Jugulare
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. ASOM (Acute Suppurative Otitis Media): This condition typically presents with rapid onset ear pain, fever, and hearing loss . The presentation of pulsatile tinnitus, a reddish tympanic membrane, and a bruit over the mastoid are not characteristic of ASOM.
  • • Option A. ASOM (Acute Suppurative Otitis Media):
  • • rapid onset
  • • ear pain, fever,
  • • hearing loss
  • • Option C. Acoustic Neuroma (Vestibular Schwannoma): This is a benign tumor of the cranial nerve VIII ( vestibulocochlear nerve ). It typically presents with gradual hearing loss and tinnitus but does not produce a reddish tympanic membrane or an audible bruit. Also, acoustic neuroma usually causes sensorineural hearing loss, not conductive hearing loss.
  • • Option C. Acoustic Neuroma (Vestibular Schwannoma):
  • • benign tumor
  • • cranial nerve VIII
  • • vestibulocochlear nerve
  • • gradual hearing loss
  • • tinnitus
  • • sensorineural hearing loss,
  • • Option D. Otosclerosis: Here, conductive hearing loss with intact tympanic membrane is noted but tinnitus is seen in only few people. And tinnitus, if present, is not pulsatile. Aquino sign is not seen here.
  • • Option D. Otosclerosis:

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Glomus jugulare in patients presenting with pulsatile tinnitus, conductive hearing loss (as indicated by negative Rinne and lateralization of Weber to the affected side ), a reddish tympanic membrane , and an audible bruit over the mastoid .
  • ➤ Glomus jugulare
  • ➤ pulsatile tinnitus, conductive hearing loss
  • ➤ negative Rinne and lateralization of Weber
  • ➤ affected side
  • ➤ reddish tympanic membrane
  • ➤ audible bruit
  • ➤ mastoid
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 109
  • ➤ :
  • ➤ P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 109

subject: ENT
question: A 25-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of chronic ear infections and persistent conductive hearing loss. On examination, the otolaryngologist suspects ossicular chain disruption. Which of the following sequences correctly represents the anatomical order of the ossicles in the middle ear from the tympanic membrane to the oval window?
options: Incus, malleus, stapes
Stapes, incus, malleus
Malleus, incus, stapes
Stapes, malleus, incus

explanation.short: C. Malleus, incus, stapes
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Malleus, incus, stapes

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Incus, malleus, stapes: This sequence is incorrect because the malleus, not the incus, is the first ossicle in the chain directly attached to the tympanic membrane.
  • • Option A. Incus, malleus, stapes:
  • • malleus, not the incus, is the first ossicle in the chain
  • • Option B. Stapes, incus, malleus: This sequence is incorrect as it reverses the actual anatomical order of the ossicles. The correct sequence starts with the malleus attached to the tympanic membrane and ends with the stapes at the oval window.
  • • Option B. Stapes, incus, malleus:
  • • reverses the actual anatomical order of the ossicles.
  • • Option D. Stapes, malleus, incus: This sequence is also incorrect because it does not follow the natural order of vibration transmission from the tympanic membrane to the oval window.
  • • Option D. Stapes, malleus, incus:
  • • does not follow the natural order of vibration transmission

Educational Objective:

subject: ENT
question: A 50-year-old woman presents with pulsatile tinnitus, hearing loss, and hoarseness. Otoscopy reveals a reddish mass behind a perforated tympanic membrane. Imaging shows invasion of the mastoid, jugular foramen, and extension toward the nasopharynx. Which of the following statements about the spread of the suspected tumor is true?
options: It commonly metastasizes to liver and kidneys in over 10% of cases
It can spread intracranially to posterior and middle cranial fossae
It never involves the eustachian tube or nasopharynx
It originates from the cochlea and spreads to the tympanic membrane

explanation.short: B. It can spread intracranially to posterior and middle cranial fossae
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) It can spread intracranially to posterior and middle cranial fossae

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Glomus tumors may spread locally to the middle ear, mastoid, skull base, nasopharynx, and intracranial fossae, and rarely metastasize to lungs or bones (4%).

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old woman presents with right-sided pulsatile tinnitus, slowly progressive hearing loss, and occasional dizziness. She also complains of mild hoarseness and difficulty swallowing. Which of the following is not true regarding the most likely diagnosis?
options: It typically presents with pulsatile tinnitus and conductive hearing loss
Brown’s sign is usually negative
Cranial nerves IX to XII may be involved in late stages
The tumor may produce catecholamines, causing systemic symptoms

explanation.short: B. Brown’s sign is usually negative
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Brown’s sign is usually negative

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Glomus tumors commonly cause pulsatile tinnitus and conductive hearing loss, show positive Brown’s sign, may involve lower cranial nerves in late stages, and can occasionally secrete catecholamines.

question: Which of the following best differentiates Ménière’s disease from Ménière’s syndrome?

Ménière’s syndrome is idiopathic, while Ménière’s disease has a known cause
Ménière’s disease is idiopathic, while Ménière’s syndrome is secondary to an identifiable cause

explanation.short: C. Ménière’s disease is idiopathic, while Ménière’s syndrome is secondary to an identifiable cause

Two or more episodes of vertigo lasting more than 20 minutes, hearing loss on audiometry, tinnitus or fullness, and exclusion of other causes

subject: ENT
question: A NEET-PG aspirant is revising the anatomy of the internal auditory canal (IAC). Which of the following correctly describes the contents and landmarks of the IAC?
options: Cochlear nerve lies in the posterosuperior quadrant of the IAC
Facial nerve lies in the anterosuperior quadrant of the IAC
Inferior vestibular nerve lies in the anterosuperior quadrant of the IAC
Bill’s bar separates superior and inferior vestibular nerves

explanation.short: B. Facial nerve lies in the anterosuperior quadrant of the IAC
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Facial nerve lies in the anterosuperior quadrant of the IAC

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Facial nerve lies in the anterosuperior quadrant of the internal auditory canal and is separated from the superior vestibular nerve by Bill’s bar.
  • ➤ Ref:-P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 128
  • ➤ Ref:-P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 128

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements about Eustachian tube function tests is correct?
options: Toynbee’s test uses positive pressure to assess Eustachian tube patency
Saccharine test evaluates the ventilatory function of the Eustachian tube
Tympanometry evaluates the ability of the tube to equilibrate pressure during swallowing
Valsalva test should always be used in children to diagnose tube dysfunction

explanation.short: C. Tympanometry evaluates the ability of the tube to equilibrate pressure during swallowing
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Tympanometry evaluates the ability of the tube to equilibrate pressure during swallowing

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Tympanometry evaluates the Eustachian tube's ability to equilibrate middle ear pressure during swallowing by assessing tympanic membrane compliance.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 59
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 59

subject: ENT
question: A 1-year-old child presents with recurrent episodes of otitis media. The pediatrician advises the mother to feed the child in an upright position. What is the most likely anatomical reason for this advice?
options: Infant Eustachian tube is narrower and longer
Infant Eustachian tube is more angulated and rigid
Infant Eustachian tube is shorter, wider, and more horizontal
Infant Eustachian tube has more elastic recoil and better closure

explanation.short: C. Infant Eustachian tube is shorter, wider, and more horizontal
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Infant Eustachian tube is shorter, wider, and more horizontal

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Infant Eustachian tubes are shorter, wider, and more horizontal, making them prone to regurgitation and otitis media; upright feeding can help prevent this.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 58
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 58

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 58

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following is not a function of the Eustachian tube?
options: Clearance of middle ear secretions via mucociliary action
Protection of middle ear from nasopharyngeal pressure and secretions
Equalization of pressure across tympanic membrane
Continuous drainage of perilymph from the inner ear

explanation.short: D. Continuous drainage of perilymph from the inner ear
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Continuous drainage of perilymph from the inner ear

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The Eustachian tube ventilates the middle ear, protects it from nasopharyngeal secretions and pressure, and clears secretions via mucociliary action.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 58
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 58

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 58

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements about the Eustachian tube is correct?
options: It develops from the first branchial cleft and is 36 cm long
The isthmus is its widest part and lies at the nasopharyngeal end
It makes a 45-degree angle with the horizontal and opens into the nasopharynx at the torus tubarius
It is 24 mm long, entirely cartilaginous, and connects middle ear to oropharynx

explanation.short: C. It makes a 45-degree angle with the horizontal and opens into the nasopharynx at the torus tubarius
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) It makes a 45-degree angle with the horizontal and opens into the nasopharynx at the torus tubarius

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The Eustachian tube is 36 mm long, develops from the first pharyngeal pouch, forms a 45° angle with the horizontal, and opens into the nasopharynx at the torus tubarius.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 57
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 57

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy complains of hearing her own voice and breathing sounds loudly in one ear. On otoscopy, tympanic membrane movements are noted with respiration. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Otitis media with effusion
Patulous Eustachian tube
Acute Eustachian tube obstruction
Tympanic membrane perforation

explanation.short: B. Patulous Eustachian tube
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Patulous Eustachian tube

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Patulous Eustachian tube presents with autophony and visible tympanic membrane movement with respiration, and is often seen in pregnancy or after weight loss.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 57
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 57

subject: ENT
question: A 6-year-old child is brought by his parents with complaints of poor attention in class and delayed speech development. There is no pain or fever. Otoscopy reveals a dull, retracted tympanic membrane with visible air bubbles behind it. Audiometry shows a conductive hearing loss of around 30 dB. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediate myringotomy with grommet insertion
Empirical antibiotic therapy for 5 days
Start medical treatment with decongestants and encourage Valsalva manoeuvre
Cortical mastoidectomy and tympanotomy

explanation.short: C. Start medical treatment with decongestants and encourage Valsalva manoeuvre
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Start medical treatment with decongestants and encourage Valsalva manoeuvre

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Otitis media with effusion in children is managed initially with decongestants, antiallergics, and maneuvers to ventilate the middle ear; surgery is considered if medical management fails.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 65
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 65

subject: ENT
question: A 6-year-old boy is brought for a school health check-up. Otoscopic examination reveals a pearly white mass visible behind an intact tympanic membrane, and the child has mild conductive hearing loss. There is no history of ear discharge or previous ear infection. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acquired cholesteatoma
Otitis media with effusion
Congenital cholesteatoma
Tympanosclerosis

explanation.short: C. Congenital cholesteatoma
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Congenital cholesteatoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Congenital cholesteatoma presents as a white mass behind an intact tympanic membrane and causes conductive hearing loss, commonly discovered incidentally in children.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 67
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 67

subject: ENT
question: A 7-year-old child presents with a history of chronic ear discharge, high-grade fever, torticollis, and a tender swelling in the upper part of the neck deep to the sternocleidomastoid muscle. The clinician suspects an abscess arising due to mastoid disease tracking through the mastoid tip. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Postauricular abscess
Bezold abscess
Zygomatic abscess
Luc abscess

explanation.short: B. Bezold abscess
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Bezold abscess

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Bezold abscess is a deep neck abscess secondary to mastoiditis, characterized by pus tracking from the mastoid tip into the neck, presenting with neck swelling, torticollis, and systemic signs.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 78
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 78

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the comparison between canal wall up and canal wall down mastoidectomy procedures?
options: Canal wall up procedure has a lower rate of residual disease and does not require a second-look surgery
Canal wall down procedure provides a normal meatal appearance and allows unrestricted swimming
Canal wall up procedure preserves a normal meatus and is better suited for patients requiring hearing aids
Canal wall down procedure requires second-look surgery to rule out cholesteatoma recurrence

explanation.short: C. Canal wall up procedure preserves a normal meatus and is better suited for patients requiring hearing aids
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Canal wall up procedure preserves a normal meatus and is better suited for patients requiring hearing aids

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Canal wall up mastoidectomy maintains normal meatal anatomy and allows easier use of hearing aids, but has a higher risk of residual disease, while canal wall down is safer for disease clearance but has limitations in swimming and hearing aid fitting.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 73
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 73

subject: ENT
question: A 14-year-old child presents with persistent ear discharge, deep-seated retro-orbital pain, and diplopia. On examination, the child has lateral rectus palsy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute mastoiditis
Bezold abscess
Petrositis
Labyrinthitis

explanation.short: C. Petrositis
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Petrositis

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Petrositis classically presents as Gradenigo syndrome with VIth nerve palsy, retro-orbital pain, and persistent otorrhea, due to infection spreading to the petrous apex along pneumatized tracts.
  • ➤ Petrositis
  • ➤ Gradenigo syndrome
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 79
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 79

subject: ENT
question: A 40-year-old man with chronic ear discharge presents with sudden onset vertigo, nausea, and vomiting. Examination reveals spontaneous nystagmus with the quick component toward the affected side. Audiometry shows mild sensorineural hearing loss. Which of the following best describes the underlying condition and its appropriate management?
options: Suppurative labyrinthitis; immediate surgical drainage and systemic corticosteroids
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo; canalith repositioning and vestibular suppressants
Meniere's disease; sodium restriction and betahistine therapy
Diffuse serous labyrinthitis; systemic antibiotics and possible mastoidectomy if conservative measures fail

explanation.short: D. Diffuse serous labyrinthitis; systemic antibiotics and possible mastoidectomy if conservative measures fail
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Diffuse serous labyrinthitis; systemic antibiotics and possible mastoidectomy if conservative measures fail

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Diffuse serous labyrinthitis is a reversible intralabyrinthine inflammation presenting with vertigo, nausea, and SNHL, and is managed with antibiotics, vestibular sedatives, and mastoidectomy if required.
  • ➤ Diffuse serous labyrinthitis
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 80
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 80

subject: ENT
question: A 28-year-old man with a history of chronic ear discharge presents with headache, fever, neck stiffness, and focal neurological deficits including hemiplegia and aphasia. On examination, he has papilledema and a dilated pupil. MRI shows pus collection between the dura and arachnoid. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediate lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
High-dose oral antibiotics and observation
Craniotomy and intravenous antibiotics
Cortical mastoidectomy followed by conservative treatment

explanation.short: C. Craniotomy and intravenous antibiotics
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Craniotomy and intravenous antibiotics

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Subdural abscess from otitis media presents with meningeal signs, focal deficits, and raised intracranial pressure, and requires urgent neurosurgical drainage and intravenous antibiotics ; lumbar puncture is contraindicated.
  • ➤ Subdural abscess
  • ➤ urgent neurosurgical drainage and intravenous antibiotics
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 81
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 81

@dams_new_robot

subject: ENT
question: A 28-year-old male with a history of chronic suppurative otitis media and recent onset of profuse ear discharge presents with high-grade fever, chills, and rigors. On examination, he has tenderness over the posterior mastoid region (Griesinger’s sign) and papilledema. Contrast-enhanced CT shows a “delta sign” in the posterior cranial fossa. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start oral antibiotics and schedule outpatient follow-up
Perform cortical or modified radical mastoidectomy with sinus exposure and IV antibiotics
Proceed directly with ligation of internal jugular vein
Start anticoagulants and observe for resolution

explanation.short: B. Perform cortical or modified radical mastoidectomy with sinus exposure and IV antibiotics
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Perform cortical or modified radical mastoidectomy with sinus exposure and IV antibiotics

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Lateral sinus thrombophlebitis is a serious intracranial complication of otitis media and requires urgent surgical drainage via mastoidectomy along with intravenous antibiotics; Griesinger's sign and "delta sign" on imaging support the diagnosis.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 85
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 85

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: - P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 85

subject: ENT
question: A 65-year-old man presents with unilateral hearing loss. Otoscopic examination reveals a dull tympanic membrane and fluid behind it, suggestive of otitis media with effusion (OME). What should be the next best step in management?
options: Prescribe antihistamines and decongestants
Evaluate the nasopharynx to rule out carcinoma
Insert a grommet after myringotomy
Perform tympanoplasty

explanation.short: B. Evaluate the nasopharynx to rule out carcinoma
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Evaluate the nasopharynx to rule out carcinoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Unilateral otitis media with effusion in an elderly patient should prompt evaluation for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, especially in the Fossa of Rosenmüller.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 251
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 251

subject: ENT
question: A 12-year-old child with a history of chronic otitis media presents with deep retro-orbital pain, diplopia, and persistent ear discharge. Which of the following conditions is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bezold's abscess
Gradenigo’s syndrome
Mastoiditis
Cholesteatoma

explanation.short: B. Gradenigo’s syndrome
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Graden go’s syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Gradenigo’s syndrome, seen in petrositis, presents with retro-orbital pain (CN V), diplopia (CN VI), and persistent otorrhea and is managed surgically.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 94

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following is NOT a standard indication for performing a postaural (Wilde’s) incision in ENT surgery?
options: Cortical mastoidectomy
Tympanoplasty for anterior perforation
Grommet insertion in otitis media with effusion
Exposure of vertical segment of facial nerve

explanation.short: C. Grommet insertion in otitis media with effusion
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Grommet insertion in otitis media with effusion

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Postaural (Wilde’s) incision is used in mastoid surgeries, tympanoplasty with anterior perforations, and to expose the vertical facial nerve segment—not for simple transcanal procedures like grommet insertion.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 401
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 401
