subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements correctly describes Schuller’s view in radiological imaging of the temporal bone?
options: It uses a 15° cephalocaudal beam and does not show the attic or antrum clearly
It is primarily used to assess nasopharyngeal tumors extending into the Eustachian tube
It shows the key area of the mastoid including antrum and upper attic, using a 30° cephalocaudal beam
The sagittal plane is perpendicular to the film and the internal and external auditory canals are separated

explanation.short: C. It shows the key area of the mastoid including antrum and upper attic, using a 30° cephalocaudal beam
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) It shows the key area of the mastoid including antrum and upper attic, using a 30° cephalocaudal beam

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Schuller’s view uses a 30° cephalocaudal beam with the sagittal plane parallel to the film and is useful for visualizing the mastoid antrum, attic, and sinodural angle, making it ideal for assessing mastoiditis, cholesteatoma, and petrous fractures.
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 433
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 433

subject: ENT
question: The instrument shown in the image is a myringotome. Which of the following statements regarding this instrument and its use is correct?
options: It is used to remove granulation tissue from the middle ear in CSOM
It is used to incise the tympanic membrane during myringotomy
It is a curette used to excise the adenoids
It is used to raise the tympanomeatal flap in tympanoplasty

explanation.short: B. It is used to incise the tympanic membrane during myringotomy
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) It is used to incise the tympanic membrane during myringotomy

subject: ENT
question: The instrument shown in the image is Jansen’s self-retaining mastoid retractor. Which of the following statements regarding this instrument is correct?
options: It is used for nasal septum elevation during septoplasty
It is manually held throughout mastoid surgery to retract soft tissues
It is self-retaining and commonly used to retract soft tissue flaps during mastoidectomy
It is used to excise granulation tissue from the middle ear cavity

explanation.short: C. It is self-retaining and commonly used to retract soft tissue flaps during mastoidectomy

Ans. C) It is self-retaining and commonly used to retract soft tissue flaps during mastoidectomy

subject: ENT
question: The instrument shown in the image is a mastoid gouge. Which of the following statements about its use is correct?
options: It is used to elevate the tympanomeatal flap in tympanoplasty
It is used to suction fluid from the middle ear during myringotomy
It is used to remove bone during mastoid surgery and comes in various sizes
It is primarily used to cauterize granulation tissue in the ear canal

Ans. C) It is used to remove bone during mastoid surgery and comes in various sizes

subject: ENT
question: Identify the instrument shown in the image and choose the correct statement regarding its surgical use:
options: Freer’s elevator – used to elevate nasal septal flaps
Farabeuf periosteal elevator – used to elevate periosteum from mastoid cortex in mastoidectomy
Lempert’s endaural speculum – used to spread the external auditory canal during endaural approach
Mollison’s mastoid retractor – used to retract postaural soft tissues during mastoid surgery

explanation.short: B. Farabeuf periosteal elevator – used to elevate periosteum from mastoid cortex in mastoidectomy

subject: ENT
question: A 5-year-old child presents with complaints of fever, severe earache, vomiting, and reduced hearing. Otoscopic examination reveals a bulging, red tympanic membrane with loss of landmarks, and a yellow spot is visible. What stage of Acute Suppurative Otitis Media (ASOM) is this child most likely in?
options: Stage of tubal occlusion
Stage of presuppuration
Stage of suppuration
Stage of resolution

explanation.short: C. Stage of suppuration
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Stage of suppuration

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The suppurative stage of ASOM is marked by bulging tympanic membrane, yellow spot, severe earache, and high fever , indicating accumulation of pus in the middle ear.
  • ➤ suppurative stage of ASOM
  • ➤ bulging tympanic membrane, yellow spot, severe earache, and high fever
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 62
  • ➤ Ref:- P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 62

subject: ENT
question: A 25-year-old man experiences sudden severe ear pain, hearing loss, and a sense of fullness in the right ear during rapid descent on a flight. Otoscopy reveals a congested, retracted tympanic membrane, and audiometry shows conductive hearing loss. What is the most likely underlying mechanism of this condition?
options: Acute bacterial infection of the middle ear
Inflammation of cochlear membranes due to viral labyrinthitis
Failure of Eustachian tube to equalize middle ear pressure during descent
Serous otitis media due to allergic rhinitis

explanation.short: C. Failure of Eustachian tube to equalize middle ear pressure during descent
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Failure of Eustachian tube to equalize middle ear pressure during descent

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Aero-otitis media occurs when the Eustachian tube fails to equalize middle ear pressure during descent, leading to TM retraction, pain, and conductive hearing loss.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following cartilages of the larynx is unpaired?
options: Cuneiform Cartilage
Epiglottis
Corniculate Cartilage
Arytenoid Cartilage

explanation.short: B. Epiglottis
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Epiglottis

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Laryngeal framework is made up of 6 cartilages – 3 unpaired (Thyroid, Cricoid, Epiglottis) & 3 paired (Arytenoid, Corniculate, Cuneiform).
  • • 6 cartilages
  • • 3 unpaired
  • • 3 paired
  • • Option A . These are paired cartilages located in the aryepiglottic fold . They are small and elongated , helping to support the folds and the laryngeal structure but are not unpaired.
  • • Option A
  • • aryepiglottic fold
  • • small
  • • elongated
  • • Option C. These are paired, small horn-like cartilages that sit on the apex of each arytenoid cartilage . They are involved in the movement and function of the vocal cords and are not unpaired.
  • • Option C.
  • • small horn-like cartilages
  • • arytenoid cartilage
  • • Option D . These are also paired cartilages, critical for vocal cord movement. They are pyramid-shaped and located at the posterior part of the larynx.
  • • Option D
  • • pyramid-shaped
  • • posterior part

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Epiglottis is the unpaired cartilage in the larynx, which functions as a protective structure to prevent the entry of food and liquid into the trachea during swallowing.
  • ➤ Epiglottis
  • ➤ protective structure

subject: ENT
question: A 50-year-old patient with a suspected laryngeal tumor undergoes imaging studies. The radiologist discusses the involvement of the pre-epiglottic space. Understanding the anatomical boundaries of this space is crucial for assessing tumor spread. Which of the following best describes the boundaries of the pre-epiglottic space?
options: Anteriorly by the thyroid cartilage, posteriorly by the cricoid cartilage, superiorly by the hyoid bone, and inferiorly by the vocal cords.
Superiorly by the hyoepiglottic ligament and hyoid bone, posteriorly by the epiglottis, and inferiorly by the thyroepiglottic ligament.
Anteriorly by the epiglottis, posteriorly by the arytenoid cartilages, superiorly by the thyroid cartilage, and inferiorly by the cricothyroid membrane.
Posteriorly by the epiglottis, anterosuperiorly by the thyrohyoid membrane, superiorly by the hyoid bone and hyoepiglottic ligament, and anteroinferiorly by the thyroid cartilage.

explanation.short: D. Posteriorly by the epiglottis, anterosuperiorly by the thyrohyoid membrane, superiorly by the hyoid bone and hyoepiglottic ligament, and anteroinferiorly by the thyroid cartilage.
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . This option incorrectly identifies the cricoid cartilage and vocal cords as posterior and inferior boundaries , which are not part of the pre-epiglottic space boundaries.

  • • Option B
  • • Option C . This option mistakenly identifies the arytenoid cartilages and cricothyroid membrane as boundaries, which are not correct for the pre-epiglottic space.
  • • Option C

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old patient with laryngeal carcinoma undergoes a CT scan for staging purposes. Understanding the anatomical boundaries of the paraglottic space is essential for assessing the extent of tumor invasion. Which of the following best describes the boundaries of the paraglottic space?
options: Anteriorly by the thyroid cartilage, posteriorly by the arytenoid cartilages, superiorly by the hyoid bone, and inferiorly by the cricoid cartilage.
Anteriorly by the thyroid cartilage, posteriorly by the cricoarytenoid joint, superiorly by the conus elasticus, and inferiorly by the cricoid cartilage.
Anteriorly by the epiglottis, posteriorly by the arytenoid cartilages, superiorly by the hyoid bone, and inferiorly by the cricoid cartilage.
Laterally by the thyroid cartilage and cricothyroid membrane, medially by conus elasticus, ventricle and quadrangular membrane, posteriorly by anterior mucosa of pyriform fossa.

explanation.short: D. Laterally by the thyroid cartilage and cricothyroid membrane, medially by conus elasticus, ventricle and quadrangular membrane, posteriorly by anterior mucosa of pyriform fossa.
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . This option incorrectly includes the hyoid bone and arytenoid cartilages as part of the paraglottic space boundaries.
  • • Option A
  • • Option B . This option incorrectly identifies the thyroid and cricoid cartilages. It inaccurately includes the cricoarytenoid joint and conus elasticus as the posterior and superior boundaries, respectively.
  • • Option B
  • • Option C . This option mistakenly identifies the epiglottis and hyoid bone as boundaries, which are not correct for the paraglottic space.
  • • Option C

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ The boundaries of the paraglottic space - Laterally thyroid cartilage and cricothyroid membrane, medially conus elasticus, ventricle and quadrangular membrane, posteriorly anterior mucosa of pyriform fossa, which is significant in assessing the spread of laryngeal carcinoma.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg285

subject: ENT
question: A 45-year-old male, a professional singer, presents with complaints of a progressive swelling in the neck and a change in voice quality over the past few months. He denies any respiratory distress. On examination, a soft, compressible mass is palpated in the neck that increases in size with Valsalva maneuver. Laryngoscopy reveals an air-filled dilation of the laryngeal saccule. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Thyroglossal duct cyst
Branchial cleft cyst
Laryngocele
Cystic hygroma

explanation.short: C. Laryngocele
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Laryngocele

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Thyroglossal duct cyst are developmental cysts that usually present in the midline of the neck. They move with swallowing or protrusion of the tongue. However, they are not air-filled and do not increase in size with the Valsalva maneuver.
  • • Option A
  • • developmental cysts
  • • midline
  • • swallowing
  • • protrusion
  • • Option B . Branchial cleft cyst are congenital epithelial cysts arising on the lateral part of the neck from the remnants of the branchial apparatus . They do not typically change in size with maneuvers like Valsalva.
  • • Option B
  • • congenital epithelial cysts
  • • lateral part
  • • remnants
  • • branchial apparatus
  • • Option D . Cystic hygroma is a congenital lymphatic malformation typically presenting in infancy or early childhood . It's a fluid-filled lesion , not air-filled, and doesn't change size with Valsalva maneuver.
  • • Option D
  • • congenital lymphatic malformation
  • • infancy
  • • early childhood
  • • fluid-filled lesion

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Laryngocele is an air-filled dilation of the laryngeal saccule that can increase in size with increased intrathoracic pressure , such as during the Valsalva maneuver, often seen in individuals who frequently increase their intrathoracic pressure.
  • ➤ air-filled dilation
  • ➤ laryngeal saccule
  • ➤ increase
  • ➤ size
  • ➤ increased intrathoracic pressure

subject: ENT
question: A 40-year-old female patient presents with a chronic, rough, and low-pitched voice. She reports no pain or discomfort but is concerned about the persistent change in her voice quality. Laryngoscopic examination reveals excessive tension and compression of the false vocal cords with minimal involvement of the true vocal cords. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Vocal Cord Nodules
Laryngeal Papillomatosis
Dysphonia Plica Ventricularis
Reinke's Edema

explanation.short: C. Dysphonia Plica Ventricularis
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Vocal cord nodules are small , bilateral lesions that form at the midpoint of the vocal cords due to chronic vocal abuse or misuse . They typically lead to a hoarse voice , but the presentation includes the use of the true vocal cords , not the false ones.

  • • Option B . Laryngeal papillomatosis condition is characterized by the growth of benign tumors on the vocal cords and other parts of the airway. It leads to a hoarse voice and breathing difficulties , but the key feature is the presence of papillomas , not just tension in the false vocal cords.
  • • Option B
  • • Laryngeal papillomatosis condition
  • • characterized
  • • growth
  • • benign tumors
  • • vocal cords
  • • leads
  • • hoarse voice
  • • breathing difficulties
  • • presence of papillomas
  • • Option D . Reinke's edema condition involves the swelling of the vocal cords due to fluid accumulation in Reinke's space . It is often associated with smoking and leads to a deeper, huskier voice . However, it primarily affects the true vocal cords , not the false ones.
  • • Option D

  • • primarily affects

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old male presents with progressive dysphagia, hoarseness, and a recent history of unintentional weight loss. Upon examination, an irregular mass is identified on the aryepiglottic fold. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma. Which of the following is the most likely complication associated with a tumor in this location?
options: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Palsy
Aspiration Pneumonia
Superior Vena Cava Syndrome
Tracheoesophageal Fistula

explanation.short: B. Aspiration Pneumonia
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy complication is more commonly associated with tumors affecting the recurrent laryngeal nerve directly , typically seen in thyroid malignancies or esophageal cancer .
  • • Option A
  • • recurrent laryngeal nerve directly
  • • thyroid malignancies
  • • esophageal cancer
  • • Option C . Superior vena cava syndrome is typically associated with tumors in the mediastinum , such as lung cancer or lymphoma , which compress the superior vena cava , not with laryngeal or pharyngeal tumors.
  • • Option C
  • • mediastinum
  • • lung cancer
  • • lymphoma
  • • compress
  • • superior vena cava
  • • Option D . Tracheoesophageal fistula - Although this can occur with esophageal cancers , it is less likely with a primary tumor in the aryepiglottic fold , unless it extensively invades surrounding structures.
  • • Option D
  • • esophageal cancers
  • • aryepiglottic fold

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ The clinical implications of a tumor in the aryepiglottic fold , particularly the increased risk of aspiration pneumonia due to impaired protective laryngeal reflexes .

subject: ENT
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with hoarseness of voice. Upon laryngoscopic examination, the otolaryngologist assesses the position and movement of her vocal cords. Which of the following positions of the vocal cords is associated with normal phonation?

  • • Explanation:

  • • Incorrect
  • • vocal cords are moved apart
  • • respiration to allow air to pass through the larynx
  • • Option C. Intermediate : Incorrect . An intermediate position is neither fully abducted nor fully adducted . It does not typically correspond to normal phonation, as this requires the vocal cords to be closer together to allow for vibration and sound generation.
  • • Option C. Intermediate
  • • Incorrect
  • • neither fully abducted nor fully adducted
  • • Option D. Paramedian : Incorrect . The paramedian position is a near-adducted position of the vocal cords but is not fully adducted. It is often seen in vocal cord paralysis where the affected cord cannot fully adduct to the midline, which may result in a breathy voice or incomplete closure and is not the position for normal phonation.
  • • Option D. Paramedian
  • • Incorrect

  • • not fully adducted.
  • • seen in vocal cord paralysis

subject: ENT
question: All are features of Infant Larynx except?
options: Laryngeal Cartilages are Soft
Larynx is Cylindrical in Shape
Larynx is positioned High
Epiglottis is Omega Shaped

explanation.short: B. Larynx is Cylindrical in Shape
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Laryngeal Cartilages are Soft
  • • True
  • • In infants, the cartilages are more pliable and soft . This makes them more prone to collapse during inspiration (as seen in laryngomalacia ). The flexibility also contributes to the floppiness of the supraglottic structures.
  • • In infants, the cartilages are more pliable and soft .
  • • pliable
  • • soft
  • • This makes them more prone to collapse during inspiration (as seen in laryngomalacia ).
  • • collapse
  • • laryngomalacia
  • • The flexibility also contributes to the floppiness of the supraglottic structures.
  • • floppiness
  • • Larynx is Positioned High
  • • True
  • • The infant larynx lies higher in the neck: typically at C1–C2 level , compared to C3–C6 in adults. This high position allows infants to breathe and feed simultaneously , which is essential during early life. Clinically important during intubation – the larynx is more anterior and cephalad .
  • • The infant larynx lies higher in the neck: typically at C1–C2 level , compared to C3–C6 in adults.
  • • C1–C2 level
  • • C3–C6
  • • This high position allows infants to breathe and feed simultaneously , which is essential during early life.
  • • high position
  • • breathe and feed simultaneously
  • • Clinically important during intubation – the larynx is more anterior and cephalad .
  • • anterior and cephalad
  • • Epiglottis is Omega Shaped
  • • True
  • • The epiglottis in infants is narrow, elongated, and omega (Ω) shaped . It is often stiff and curled , which can make visualization during laryngoscopy more difficult in newborns and infants.
  • • The epiglottis in infants is narrow, elongated, and omega (Ω) shaped .
  • • narrow, elongated, and omega (Ω) shaped
  • • It is often stiff and curled , which can make visualization during laryngoscopy more difficult in newborns and infants.
  • • stiff and curled
  • • visualization during laryngoscopy

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ The infant larynx differs from the adult larynx by being conical (not cylindrical), positioned higher (C1–C2), with a soft cartilage framework and omega-shaped epiglottis.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following structures is NOT removed in radical neck dissection?
options: Parotid Gland
Internal Jugular Vein
XI Cranial Nerve
Sternocleido Mastoid

explanation.short: A. Parotid Gland
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation: Radical neck dissection is a surgical procedure performed primarily for the treatment of advanced head and neck cancers with lymph node metastasis. It involves the removal of various structures within the neck to ensure comprehensive clearance of potentially involved lymph nodes. Understanding the structures removed and preserved is essential:
  • • - Option A: Parotid gland - The parotid gland is not typically removed in a standard radical neck dissection unless specifically indicated due to local invasion. The procedure primarily focuses on the removal of lymph nodes, the sternocleidomastoid muscle, the internal jugular vein, and the spinal accessory nerve. Only the tail of the parotid may be included if it is involved, but the entire gland is not routinely excised.
  • • - Option A: Parotid gland - The parotid gland is not typically removed in a standard radical neck dissection unless specifically indicated due to local invasion. The procedure primarily focuses on the removal of lymph nodes, the sternocleidomastoid muscle, the internal jugular vein, and the spinal accessory nerve. Only the tail of the parotid may be included if it is involved, but the entire gland is not routinely excised.
  • • - Option B: Internal jugular vein - The internal jugular vein is removed during a radical neck dissection to provide adequate clearance of level II to IV lymph nodes and to reduce the chances of residual disease.
  • • - Option B: Internal jugular vein - The internal jugular vein is removed during a radical neck dissection to provide adequate clearance of level II to IV lymph nodes and to reduce the chances of residual disease.
  • • - Option C: XI cranial nerve (Spinal Accessory Nerve) - The spinal accessory nerve is resected to ensure complete removal of lymphatic drainage from levels II and III. This nerve innervates the trapezius muscle, and its removal can result in shoulder dysfunction (accessory nerve palsy).
  • • - Option C: XI cranial nerve (Spinal Accessory Nerve) - The spinal accessory nerve is resected to ensure complete removal of lymphatic drainage from levels II and III. This nerve innervates the trapezius muscle, and its removal can result in shoulder dysfunction (accessory nerve palsy).
  • • - Option D: Sternocleidomastoid muscle - The sternocleidomastoid muscle is also resected during radical neck dissection. Its removal allows better visualization and clearance of lymph node levels and decreases tumor burden.
  • • - Option D: Sternocleidomastoid muscle - The sternocleidomastoid muscle is also resected during radical neck dissection. Its removal allows better visualization and clearance of lymph node levels and decreases tumor burden.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective: In radical neck dissection, the parotid gland is typically preserved, whereas the internal jugular vein, sternocleidomastoid muscle, and spinal accessory nerve are removed.

question: A 55-year-old man with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and recent extensive neck surgery presents with respiratory distress and failure to wean from mechanical ventilation. Acute epiglottitis requiring immediate airway management
Need for bypassing upper airway obstruction due to post-surgical neck swelling
Desire for improved speech and swallowing in a patient with long-term tracheostomy

Ans. C) Need for bypassing upper airway obstruction due to post-surgical neck swelling

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Long-term mechanical ventilation and difficult weaning due to COPD : Incorrect . While tracheostomy is often considered for patients requiring long-term mechanical ventilation , the indication does not specifically necessitate a high tracheostomy over a standard one. The decision between high versus standard tracheostomy placement depends more on anatomical and surgical considerations rather than the underlying pulmonary condition.

  • • Option B. Acute epiglottitis requiring immediate airway management: Incorrect . Acute epiglottitis does necessitate immediate airway management , but tracheostomy (high or standard) is not the first-line treatment . Instead, securing the airway with intubation is typically preferred in the acute setting.
  • • Option B. Acute epiglottitis requiring immediate airway management:
  • • Incorrect
  • • necessitate immediate airway management
  • • tracheostomy
  • • not the first-line treatment
  • • Option D. Desire for improved speech and swallowing in a patient with long-term tracheostomy: Incorrect . While modifications to tracheostomy tubes and procedures can facilitate speech and swallowing , the choice of a high tracheostomy is not specifically aimed at improving these functions but rather at addressing anatomical or surgical constraints.
  • • Option D. Desire for improved speech and swallowing in a patient with long-term tracheostomy:

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old man with a history of smoking and alcohol use presents with dysphagia, weight loss, and a hoarse voice. A biopsy of a lesion in the supraglottis confirms squamous cell carcinoma. Laryngoscopic examination reveals a tumor involving the mucosa of the base of the tongue and the medial wall of the pyriform sinus without fixation of the larynx. A CT scan shows the presence of a single 4 cm lymph node metastasis in the ipsilateral neck. No distant metastasis is detected. What is the most appropriate TNM staging for this patient's carcinoma of the supraglottis?
options: T2N1M0
T2N2aM0
T3N1M0
T3N2aM0

explanation.short: B. T2N2aM0
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) T2N2aM0

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. T2N1M0: Incorrect . This would indicate a tumor that invades more than one subsite of the supraglottis without laryngeal fixation and a single ipsilateral lymph node metastasis 3 cm or less in greatest dimension , which does not match the given lymph node size.
  • • Option A. T2N1M0:
  • • Incorrect
  • • indicate a tumor
  • • invades more than one subsite of the supraglottis
  • • laryngeal fixation
  • • single ipsilateral lymph node metastasis 3 cm
  • • less in greatest dimension
  • • Option C. T3N1M0: Incorrect . T3 would suggest vocal cord fixation or invasion into post-cricoid area , pre-epiglottic tissues , paraglottic space , and/or minor thyroid cartilage invasion , which is not described in the case. N1 would be a single ipsilateral lymph node metastasis 3 cm or less in greatest dimension, which is also not consistent with the given lymph node size.
  • • Option C. T3N1M0:
  • • Incorrect
  • • T3 would suggest vocal cord fixation
  • • invasion into post-cricoid area
  • • pre-epiglottic tissues
  • • paraglottic space
  • • minor thyroid cartilage invasion
  • • Option D. T3N2aM0: Incorrect . While N2a is correct for the lymph node size , T3 is not correct for the description of the primary tumor as there is no mention of vocal cord fixation or invasion into the areas specified for T3 classification.
  • • Option
  • • D. T3N2aM0:
  • • Incorrect
  • • N2a is correct for the lymph node size

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The TNM staging of carcinoma of the supraglottis is based on the extent of the primary tumor (T), the involvement of regional lymph nodes (N), and the presence of distant metastasis (M). In this case, the appropriate staging is T2N2aM0 , indicating a tumor invading more than one adjacent subsite without laryngeal fixation , a single ipsilateral lymph node metastasis larger than 3 cm but not more than 6 cm, and no distant metastasis.

question: A 60-year-old male, a lifelong smoker, presents with a complaint of persistent hoarseness for the past four months. He denies any respiratory distress, dysphagia, or significant weight loss. The examination reveals a hoarse voice but no stridor or respiratory distress. Laryngoscopic examination shows bilateral white patches on the vocal cords. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?
options: Laryngeal Carcinoma
Leukoplakia of the Vocal Cords
Laryngeal Papillomatosis
Vocal Cord Polyps

explanation.short: B. Leukoplakia of the Vocal Cords
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Leukoplakia of the Vocal Cords.

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Laryngeal carcinoma may present with hoarseness , but typically has other symptoms like dysphagia , odynophagia , or weight loss . Laryngoscopic examination usually reveals a mass rather than white patches.
  • • Option A
  • • hoarseness
  • • dysphagia
  • • odynophagia
  • • weight loss
  • • Option C . Laryngeal papillomatosis is characterized by the presence of multiple, wart-like growths on the larynx and is most commonly caused by HPV infection . It is less common in older adults.
  • • Option C
  • • presence
  • • multiple, wart-like growths
  • • larynx
  • • HPV infection
  • • Option D . Vocal cord polyps typically present as a single, unilateral lesion causing hoarseness . They are not characterized by white patches and are often related to voice abuse or trauma.
  • • Option D
  • • single, unilateral lesion
  • • hoarseness

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Leukoplakia of the vocal cords as a condition presenting with white patches on the vocal cords , often associated with smoking , and considered a premalignant lesion.
  • ➤ white patches
  • ➤ vocal cords
  • ➤ smoking
  • ➤ premalignant lesion.
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 304
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 304

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 304

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old teacher presents the clinic with a three-week history of hoarseness. She reports no pain or respiratory symptoms but has been using her voice extensively during this period. Physical examination and laryngoscopy reveal mild edema of the vocal cords without any nodules or polyps. Understanding the microstructure of the vocal cords is essential in diagnosing and treating voice disorders. How many distinct layers constitute the vocal cord microstructure?
options: Three
Four
Five
Six

explanation.short: C. Five
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Five

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . This option is incorrect. The vocal cords have more than three layers. While three layers might be a simplified description, it does not accurately represent the detailed microstructure.
  • • Option A
  • • does not accurately represent
  • • Option B . This option is also incorrect. Although the vocal cords have multiple layers, the number is greater than four when considering the detailed microanatomy.
  • • Option B
  • • greater than four
  • • Option D . This option is incorrect. While the vocal cords are complex structures , they do not have six distinct layers in their microstructure.
  • • Option D
  • • complex structures

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The vocal cord microstructure consists of five distinct layers: the epithelium , superficial layer of the lamina propria, intermediate layer of the lamina propria, deep layer of the lamina propria, and the vocalis muscle .
  • ➤ vocal cord microstructure
  • ➤ epithelium
  • ➤ superficial layer
  • ➤ intermediate layer
  • ➤ deep layer
  • ➤ vocalis muscle
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 283-285
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 283-285

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 283-285

subject: ENT
question: A 45-year-old man presents with a history of progressive dysphagia and a sensation of a lump in his throat for the past six months. He has a history of heavy tobacco and alcohol use. On examination, an indirect laryngoscopy is performed, which shows an irregular mass in the hypopharynx. For appropriate diagnosis and management, a thorough understanding of the anatomy of the hypopharynx is crucial. Which of the following is NOT a part of the hypopharynx?
options: Pyriform Sinus
Postcricoid Area
Posterior Pharyngeal Wall
Vallecula

explanation.short: D. Vallecula
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Vallecula

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A . Pyriform Sinus is a part of the hypopharynx . The pyriform sinuses are bilateral recesses on either side of the laryngeal orifice and are important sites for the lodgment of ingested substances or pathology .
  • • Option A
  • • hypopharynx
  • • bilateral recesses
  • • either side
  • • laryngeal orifice
  • • lodgment
  • • ingested substances
  • • pathology
  • • Option B . Postcricoid area, located posterior to the cricoid cartilage , is a component of the hypopharynx and is important in swallowing .
  • • Option B
  • • posterior
  • • cricoid cartilage
  • • component
  • • hypopharynx
  • • swallowing
  • • Option C . Posterior pharyngeal wall forms the upper border of the quadrangular membrane and is part of the hypopharynx . It plays a role in the function of the larynx and hypopharynx .
  • • Option C
  • • upper border
  • • quadrangular membrane
  • • hypopharynx
  • • role
  • • function
  • • larynx
  • • hypopharynx

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The hypopharynx consists of the pyriform sinus , postcricoid area , and posterior pharyngeal wall , while the vallecula is a part of the oropharynx and not the hypopharynx.
  • ➤ pyriform sinus
  • ➤ postcricoid area
  • ➤ posterior pharyngeal wall
  • ➤ vallecula
  • ➤ oropharynx
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 241
  • ➤ Ref : P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery 6 th Edition, pg 241

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following correctly describes the composition of the external nose?
options: The external nose is entirely cartilaginous and contains only septal cartilage
The upper one-third of the external nose is bony, formed by the paired nasal bones
The lower one-third of the external nose is formed by nasal bones and vomer
The cartilaginous part includes only the upper lateral and alar cartilages

explanation.short: B. The upper one-third of the external nose is bony, formed by the paired nasal bones
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) The upper one-third of the external nose is bony, formed by the paired nasal bones

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The external nose is made up of an upper bony one-third (paired nasal bones) and a lower two-thirds cartilaginous part (including upper lateral, lower lateral/alar, lesser alar, and septal cartilages).
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 134
  • ➤ Ref:
  • ➤ P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 134

subject: ENT
question: A 58-year-old man presents with progressive thickening and enlargement of the tip of his nose over the past several years. He has a long-standing history of acne rosacea. On examination, his nasal tip appears bulbous, lobulated, and red with prominent pores. There are no signs of active infection or systemic symptoms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Nasal basal cell carcinoma
Rhinophyma
Sebaceous cyst of the nose
Nasal vestibulitis

explanation.short: B. Rhinophyma
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Rhinophyma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Rhinophyma is a complication of acne rosacea caused by sebaceous gland hypertrophy, presenting as a bulbous, lobulated enlargement of the nasal tip, typically managed by laser or surgical excision.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 134
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 134

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the nasal vestibule is correct?
options: The nasal vestibule is lined by respiratory epithelium
Furuncles of the vestibule are most commonly caused by Streptococcus pyogenes
The vestibule contains sebaceous glands and vibrissae with hair follicles
The vestibule is supplied by the anterior ethmoidal nerve

explanation.short: C. The vestibule contains sebaceous glands and vibrissae with hair follicles
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) The vestibule contains sebaceous glands and vibrissae with hair follicles

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The nasal vestibule is skin-lined and contains sebaceous glands and vibrissae; it is innervated by infraorbital nerve branches and is a common site for Staphylococcus-induced furuncles.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the nasal valve is correct?
options: The nasal valve is the widest part of the nasal cavity and aids in olfaction
Its lateral boundary is formed by the lower lateral (alar) cartilage
The angle formed between the septum and upper lateral cartilage is approximately 30 degrees
It is bounded medially by the inferior turbinate and laterally by the middle turbinate

explanation.short: C. The angle formed between the septum and upper lateral cartilage is approximately 30 degrees
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The nasal valve is the narrowest part of the nasal cavity, formed by the septum medially, floor inferiorly, and lower edge of the upper lateral cartilage laterally, with an angle of ~30° between septum and cartilage.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135
  • ➤ Ref:
  • ➤ P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the inferior turbinate is correct?
options: It is part of the ethmoid bone and lies within the middle meatus
Its mucosa contains vascular channels running anterior to posterior
It attaches anteriorly to the maxilla and posteriorly to the palatine bone
It plays no role in humidifying inspired air

explanation.short: C. It attaches anteriorly to the maxilla and posteriorly to the palatine bone
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The inferior turbinate is a separate bone attached to the maxilla and palatine bone, with posterior-to-anterior vascular channels in its mucosa aiding in humidification of inspired air.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements about the drainage of lacrimal secretions is correct?
options: The nasolacrimal duct opens into the middle meatus, guarded by the valve of Hasner
The lacrimal sac lies lateral to the medial canthus and connects directly to the inferior turbinate
Blinking activates the lacrimal pump mechanism via compression of the lacrimal canaliculi
Tear secretions are directly drained into the nasal vestibule during crying

explanation.short: C. Blinking activates the lacrimal pump mechanism via compression of the lacrimal canaliculi
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Tear secretions drain from the lacrimal sac into the inferior meatus via the nasolacrimal duct, aided by blinking-induced lacrimal pump action compressing the canaliculi.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135
  • ➤ Ref:
  • ➤ P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 135

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the middle turbinate is correct?
options: The second part of the middle turbinate lies in the sagittal plane and attaches to the cribriform plate
The ground lamella (basal lamella) separates the superior and middle meatus
The third part of the middle turbinate runs horizontally toward the posterior choana and attaches to the lamina papyracea
The agger nasi lies behind the middle turbinate attachment and is never pneumatized

explanation.short: C. The third part of the middle turbinate runs horizontally toward the posterior choana and attaches to the lamina papyracea
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The third (axial) part of the middle turbinate runs horizontally toward the posterior choana and attaches to the lamina papyracea.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 136
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 136

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the anatomy of the middle meatus is correct?
options: The uncinate process lies posterior to the ethmoidal bulla and opens directly into the suprabullar recess
The space between the uncinate process and ethmoidal bulla is called the infundibulum
The natural ostium of the maxillary sinus opens into the infundibulum in the middle meatus
Haller cells are located between the ethmoidal bulla and uncinate process within the hiatus semilunaris

explanation.short: C. The natural ostium of the maxillary sinus opens into the infundibulum in the middle meatus
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The natural ostium of the maxillary sinus opens into the infundibulum, a key space in the middle meatus that forms part of the osteomeatal unit.
  • ➤ Ref: PL Dhingra, 6th Ed.; Radiological and endoscopic sinus anatomy references.
  • ➤ Ref: PL Dhingra, 6th Ed.; Radiological and endoscopic sinus anatomy references.

subject: ENT
question: A surgeon performing Functional Endoscopic Sinus Surgery (FESS) is evaluating the uncinate process attachment to determine the drainage pathway of the frontal sinus. Which of the following attachment patterns of the uncinate process is correctly matched with the corresponding drainage route of the frontal sinus?
options: Uncinate attached to lamina papyracea → Frontal sinus drains laterally into maxillary sinus
Uncinate attached to skull base → Frontal sinus drains medially into nasal cavity
Uncinate attached to middle turbinate → Frontal sinus drains laterally into infundibulum/maxillary sinus
Uncinate attached laterally to lamina papyracea → Frontal sinus drains laterally into maxillary sinus

explanation.short: C. Uncinate attached to middle turbinate → Frontal sinus drains laterally into infundibulum/maxillary sinus
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Frontal sinus drainage depends on the site of uncinate process attachment; medial attachment to middle turbinate directs drainage laterally into the maxillary sinus.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 137
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 137

@dams_new_robot
explanation.ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 137

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the anatomy around the superior turbinate is correct?
options: The Onodi cell is a posterior ethmoidal air cell that may lie close to the optic nerve
Posterior ethmoid air cells drain into the sphenoethmoidal recess
The superior turbinate is a separate bone that marks the opening of the frontal sinus
The sphenoid sinus opens into the superior meatus, lateral to the middle turbinate

explanation.short: A. The Onodi cell is a posterior ethmoidal air cell that may lie close to the optic nerve
explanation.detail:
Ans. A)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The Onodi cell is a posterior ethmoidal air cell that lies close to the optic nerve and is important to identify during endoscopic sinus surgery.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 137
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 137

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements about the nerve supply of the lateral wall of the nasal cavity is correct?
options: The olfactory nerve supplies the posterior part of the lateral nasal wall
The sphenopalatine ganglion lies anterior to the middle turbinate in the nasal cavity
The anterior ethmoidal nerve supplies the anterior and superior part of the lateral nasal wall
Branches of the greater palatine nerve supply the superior portion of the lateral nasal wall

explanation.short: C. The anterior ethmoidal nerve supplies the anterior and superior part of the lateral nasal wall
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The anterior ethmoidal nerve supplies the anterior and superior part of the lateral nasal wall, while posterior parts are innervated by branches from the sphenopalatine ganglion.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 138

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the bulla ethmoidalis and its anatomical relations is correct?
options: The bulla ethmoidalis lies anterior to the uncinate process and forms the anterior boundary of the hiatus semilunaris
The space above or behind the bulla ethmoidalis is called the infundibulum
The bulla ethmoidalis can fuse posteriorly with the ground lamella and may extend superiorly to the skull base
The hiatus semilunaris superior lies between the uncinate process and the lamina papyracea

explanation.short: C. The bulla ethmoidalis can fuse posteriorly with the ground lamella and may extend superiorly to the skull base
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The bulla ethmoidalis lies posterior to the uncinate process, may extend to the skull base, and can fuse with the ground lamella, forming part of the lateral sinus anatomy.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the Agger nasi is correct?
options: The agger nasi lies posterior to the middle turbinate and contains the bulla ethmoidalis
An enlarged agger nasi cell may obstruct the maxillary sinus ostium
The agger nasi is an elevation anterior to the middle turbinate and may contain air cells communicating with the frontal recess
The agger nasi communicates directly with the sphenoethmoidal recess

explanation.short: C. The agger nasi is an elevation anterior to the middle turbinate and may contain air cells communicating with the frontal recess
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The agger nasi lies anterior to the middle turbinate, may be pneumatized, and communicates with the frontal recess, potentially obstructing frontal sinus drainage if enlarged.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding concha bullosa is correct?
options: Concha bullosa is a pneumatized inferior turbinate that drains into the maxillary sinus
Pneumatization of the middle turbinate resulting in concha bullosa may drain into the frontal recess
Concha bullosa refers to a cystic lesion of the middle meatus with no communication to sinuses
Concha bullosa is an abnormality of the superior turbinate and communicates with the sphenoid sinus

explanation.short: B. Pneumatization of the middle turbinate resulting in concha bullosa may drain into the frontal recess
explanation.detail:
Ans. B)

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Concha bullosa is a pneumatized middle turbinate that may drain into the frontal recess directly or via agger nasi cells, affecting frontal sinus drainage.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding Haller cells is correct?
options: Haller cells are air cells located in the floor of the nasal cavity and derived from sphenoid sinus
Haller cells arise from anterior or posterior ethmoid cells and are located in the roof of the maxillary sinus
Haller cells communicate with the frontal sinus and may cause frontal sinus obstruction
Haller cells are located in the lamina papyracea and commonly obstruct the sphenoethmoidal recess

explanation.short: B. Haller cells arise from anterior or posterior ethmoid cells and are located in the roof of the maxillary sinus

Ans. B)

  • ➤ Haller cells are infraorbital air cells derived from anterior or posterior ethmoidal cells, located in the roof of the maxillary sinus and may obstruct its drainage by encroaching on the infundibulum.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements about the superior turbinate is CORRECT?
options: The superior turbinate is part of the maxillary bone and is located anterior to the middle turbinate
The superior turbinate lies lateral to the sphenoid sinus ostium and is derived from the frontal bone
The superior turbinate is the main drainage site for the anterior ethmoid and maxillary sinuses
The superior turbinate is an ethmoturbinal, located posterior and superior to the middle turbinate, and serves as a key landmark for identifying the sphenoid sinus ostium

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the superior meatus is correct?
options: The superior meatus lies above the superior turbinate and receives drainage from the frontal sinus
The superior meatus is located below the superior turbinate and receives drainage from posterior ethmoid cells
The superior meatus lies below the middle turbinate and drains the maxillary sinus
The superior meatus receives drainage from the sphenoid sinus via the sphenoethmoidal recess

explanation.short: B. The superior meatus is located below the superior turbinate and receives drainage from posterior ethmoid cells
explanation.detail:
Ans. B)

options: Onodi cell is an anterior ethmoid air cell that communicates with the frontal sinus
Onodi cell is a posterior ethmoid cell that may extend superior or lateral to the sphenoid sinus and lie close to the optic nerve
Onodi cell lies in the floor of the maxillary sinus and is related to the infraorbital nerve
Onodi cell drains into the inferior meatus and has no surgical significance

subject: ENT

subject: ENT
question: A 27-year-old man presents with sudden onset of loss of smell and taste without nasal congestion or rhinorrhea. He has no prior history of allergies or chronic rhinitis. He tests positive for COVID-19 the next day. According to recent clinical studies on SARS-CoV-2, which of the following best explains the mechanism of anosmia in this patient?
options: Damage to sustentacular and Bowman gland cells affecting olfactory sensory neurons
Obstruction of olfactory cleft due to viral mucosal edema in inferior meatus
Primary viral infection of trigeminal sensory neurons causing signal misrouting
Destruction of mitral cells in olfactory bulb by host autoantibodies post-viral clearance

explanation.detail:
Ans. A)

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ COVID-19-related anosmia is primarily due to SARS-CoV-2-induced damage to sustentacular and Bowman gland cells, which support olfactory sensory neurons in the nasal epithelium.

question: Topical nasal decongestant abuse causes:

explanation.short: C. Rhinitis Medicamentosa
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Rhinitis Medicamentosa

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Rhinitis sicca is characterized by dryness of the nasal mucosa , which can result from various factors, including environmental conditions , medications that dry out the mucosa , and certain medical conditions. While nasal decongestant abuse can alter nasal mucosa , it primarily leads to rebound congestion rather than dryness .

  • • Option B. Atrophic rhinitis is a chronic condition characterized by thinning and hardening of the nasal mucosa , as well as the presence of crusts that can emit a foul odor . It is usually caused by chronic infection , surgical procedures , or radiation therapy , not by decongestant abuse.
  • • Option B.
  • • chronic condition
  • • thinning
  • • hardening
  • • nasal mucosa
  • • presence
  • • crusts
  • • emit
  • • foul odor
  • • chronic infection
  • • surgical procedures
  • • radiation therapy
  • • Option D. Irritative rhinitis is caused by exposure to irritants such as smoke , pollution , strong odors , or dust . While these can cause inflammation and symptoms of rhinitis , irritative rhinitis is not caused by the abuse of nasal decongestants.
  • • Option D.

question: A 40-year-old man presents with a chronic, foul-smelling nasal discharge, nasal obstruction, and a sensation of fullness in his nose.   • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Nasal polyps are benign growths that arise from the nasal mucosa or the paranasal sinuses . They typically present with symptoms of nasal obstruction , reduced sense of smell , and postnasal drip but do not have a foul-smelling discharge or a cheese-like appearance.

  • • postnasal drip

  • • Option B.
  • • dryness
  • • nasal mucosa
  • • crust
  • • formation
  • • bleeding
  • • Option D. The presence of a foreign body can cause symptoms of nasal obstruction and foul-smelling discharge due to secondary infection . However, the description of a soft, cheese-like material is more characteristic of rhinitis caseosa than a typical foreign body reaction.
  • • Option D.

  • • foul-smelling discharge
  • • secondary infection

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old woman presents with complaints of watery nasal discharge that occurs shortly after eating, especially when consuming hot or spicy foods. She has no history of allergies and her symptoms are not accompanied by sneezing or nasal itching. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Allergic Rhinitis
Vasomotor Rhinitis
Gustatory Rhinitis
Atrophic Rhinitis

explanation.short: C. Gustatory Rhinitis
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Gustatory Rhinitis

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Patients with allergic rhinitis typically present with symptoms triggered by environmental allergens , such as pollen , dust mites , or pet dander . Symptoms often include sneezing , itching , nasal congestion , and watery nasal discharge . The symptom profile does not match that described in the scenario, where symptoms are specifically triggered by eating hot or spicy foods.

  • • watery nasal discharge
  • • Option B. Vasomotor rhinitis involves a non-allergic hyper responsiveness of the nasal mucosa to various triggers, including changes in temperature , humidity , or exposure to strong odors . While symptoms can include watery nasal discharge , they are not specifically linked to the consumption of food as with gustatory rhinitis.
  • • Option B.
  • • non-allergic hyper responsiveness
  • • nasal mucosa
  • • changes
  • • temperature
  • • humidity
  • • exposure
  • • strong odors
  • • watery nasal discharge
  • • Option D. Atrophic rhinitis involves the thinning of the nasal mucosa and is characterized by a dry or crusty nose , sometimes accompanied by a foul-smelling odor . It does not typically present with watery nasal discharge triggered by food consumption.
  • • Option D.
  • • thinning
  • • nasal mucosa
  • • dry
  • • crusty nose
  • • accompanied
  • • foul-smelling odor

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Gustatory rhinitis is a condition characterized by watery nasal discharge triggered by the consumption of hot or spicy foods , without the presence of sneezing or nasal itching , distinguishing it from allergic and other forms of non-allergic rhinitis .

subject: ENT
question: A 15-year-old boy presented with excessive sneezing, running nose, nose blocks and itchy nose since 2 months and symptoms were interfering with his day to day activities and causing disturbed sleep as well. What is the diagnosis?

Mild Persistent Allergic Rhinitis
Severe Intermittent Allergic Rhinitis

explanation.short: D. Severe Persistent Allergic Rhinitis
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Severe Persistent Allergic Rhinitis

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Mild intermittent allergic rhinitis would be characterized by symptoms present less than 4 days a week or for less than 4 weeks. Since the patient's symptoms are present for more than 2 months and are affecting his daily activities and sleep, this option is not correct .
  • • Option A.
  • • not correct
  • • Option B. Patients with mild persistent allergic rhinitis would have symptoms present more than 4 days a week and for more than 4 weeks, but the symptoms do not impair sleep or daily activities. Given that the patient's sleep and daily activities are affected, this option is also incorrect .
  • • Option B.
  • • incorrect
  • • Option C. Severe intermittent allergic rhinitis: Severe symptoms may be present but would be considered intermittent if they occur less than 4 days a week or for less than 4 weeks. Since the symptoms have been present for 2 months, this option is not applicable .
  • • Option C.
  • • not applicable

subject: ENT
question: A patient with mild intermittent symptoms of allergic rhinitis is treated with:
options: Short course of oral steroids
Intranasal steroids
Antihistamines
Immunotherapy

explanation.short: C. Antihistamines
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Antihistamines

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Short course of oral steroids are generally reserved for severe or uncontrolled allergic rhinitis symptoms because of the potential for significant side effects with long-term use. They are not typically used for mild intermittent symptoms.
  • • Option A.
  • • severe
  • • uncontrolled allergic rhinitis symptoms
  • • potential
  • • significant side effects
  • • Option B. While intranasal steroids are very effective for allergic rhinitis , they are usually considered for regular use in patients with persistent symptoms rather than those with mild intermittent symptoms .

  • • regular use
  • • persistent symptoms
  • • mild intermittent symptoms
  • • Option D. Allergen immunotherapy is a treatment option for allergic rhinitis , typically considered for patients with symptoms not adequately controlled with pharmacotherapy or those with significant allergen exposure that cannot be avoided. It is not generally the first-line treatment for mild intermittent symptoms.
  • • Option D.
  • • allergic rhinitis
  • • STEP-CARE APPROACH FOR TREATMENT OF ALLERGIC RHINITIS
  • • STEP-CARE APPROACH FOR TREATMENT OF ALLERGIC RHINITIS
  • • Mild intermittent disease can be treated with oral antihistamines or intranasal Cromolyn sodium . Moderate or persistent allergic symptoms can be managed with intranasal corticosteroids as monotherapy . Severe symptoms may require combination therapy with oral non-sedating antihistamines and intranasal steroids . If severe and persistent symptoms persist despite the above treatments, a short course of oral steroids and immunotherapy is recommended. Nasal obstruction can be addressed with a short course of intranasal decongestants . Oral decongestants can be combined with antihistamines . Avoidance of allergens and irritants is advised for all forms of allergic rhinitis. Non-allergic rhinitis can coexist with allergic rhinitis , where nonspecific stimuli can cause allergic rhinitis-like symptoms due to nasal mucosa hyper-reactivity .

subject: ENT
question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with unilateral nasal obstruction and episodic nasal bleeding for the past six months. He also reports a decreased sense of smell on the affected side. Physical examination reveals a unilateral, irregular, mass in the nasal cavity, which is firm and bleeds easily upon manipulation. subject: ENT
question: A 32-year-old patient presented with a dry and roomy cavity with a foul smell in the nose. There is now a granulomatous growth in the nasal cavity. A biopsy of the growth was taken and sent for histopathological examination. Which of the following findings is most likely to be observed?
options: Russell Bodies
Mikulicz Cells
Large Macrophages with Foamy Cytoplasm
All are seen

explanation.short: D. All are seen
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) All are seen

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Russell bodies are eosinophilic inclusions within plasma cells , indicating an intense immune response , often seen in chronic inflammatory conditions . They represent accumulated immunoglobulins within the endoplasmic reticulum . In the context of a granulomatous growth in the nasal cavity , their presence might reflect a chronic inflammatory state , possibly secondary to a longstanding infection or immune-mediated process .

Mikulicz cells are distinctive large macrophages with a foamy , vacuolated appearance , classically associated with rhinoscleroma , an infection caused by Klebsiella pneumoniae subspecies rhinoscleromatis . These cells are indicative of a specific granulomatous infection and suggest a particular etiology for the nasal growth .

Large macrophages with foamy cytoplasm, often termed foam cells , are characteristic of granulomatous inflammation , which can be seen in a variety of conditions, including infections (bacterial, fungal, mycobacterial) and inflammatory diseases . Their presence indicates an ongoing response to a persistent antigenic stimulus .

This refers to the defective movement of cilia , which are microscopic , hair-like structures that line the respiratory tract , among other organs. In Kartagener syndrome, this defect leads to problems with mucus clearance , contributing to respiratory complications .

  • • Option D. Situs Inversus refers to a mirror-image arrangement of the internal organs , and it's a hallmark of Kartagener syndrome . About half of those with primary ciliary dyskinesia (and nearly all with Kartagener syndrome) have situs inversus , where organs like the heart are positioned on the opposite side of the body .

question: Treatment of Recurrent Antro-Choanal Polyp is:

explanation.short: B. FESS

Ans. B) FESS

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Caldwell-Luc Operation is an older surgical technique used for sinus surgery . It involves creating an opening in the canine fossa to access the maxillary sinus . While it can be used for removing antrochoanal polyps , it's more invasive compared to FESS and is associated with a higher risk of complications. Thus, it is not the first choice for treating recurrent antrochoanal polyps .

subject: ENT
question: A 35-year-old man presents with a history of chronic sinus pressure and occasional sharp pains behind his eyes. His symptoms are not relieved by standard sinusitis treatments. The ENT specialist decides to order a Pierre’s view (Occipito-mental with mouth open) X-ray of the paranasal sinuses (PNS) to further evaluate his sphenoid sinuses. Which of the following best describes the utility of the Pierre’s view X-ray in this patient's assessment?
options: It is primarily used to visualize the maxillary sinuses
It provides optimal visualization of the frontal sinuses
It is the preferred view for evaluating the ethmoid sinuses
It gives the best view of the sphenoid sinuses

explanation.short: D. It gives the best view of the sphenoid sinuses
explanation.detail:
Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Pierre’s view (Occipito-mental with mouth open) is a specialized radiographic projection that enables unobstructed visualization of the sphenoid sinuses by avoiding superimposition of the hard palate and mandible. This view is particularly useful for evaluating sphenoid sinus pathologies , which may present as retro-orbital or deep-seated headaches not responding to standard sinusitis therapy.
  • • Pierre’s view (Occipito-mental with mouth open) is a specialized radiographic projection that enables unobstructed visualization of the sphenoid sinuses by avoiding superimposition of the hard palate and mandible. This view is particularly useful for evaluating sphenoid sinus pathologies , which may present as retro-orbital or deep-seated headaches not responding to standard sinusitis therapy.
  • • Pierre’s view (Occipito-mental with mouth open) is a specialized radiographic projection that enables unobstructed visualization of the sphenoid sinuses by avoiding superimposition of the hard palate and mandible. This view is particularly useful for evaluating sphenoid sinus pathologies , which may present as retro-orbital or deep-seated headaches not responding to standard sinusitis therapy.
  • • Pierre’s view (Occipito-mental with mouth open)
  • • sphenoid sinuses
  • • particularly useful for evaluating sphenoid sinus pathologies

question: A 60-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled diabetes presents with facial pain, blackish nasal discharge, and fever two weeks after recovering from a severe case of COVID-19. On examination, there is black eschar on the nasal mucosa and the patient is disoriented. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the sinuses shows sinus wall destruction. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bacterial sinusitis
Allergic fungal sinusitis
Nasal cellulitis
Rhino-orbital-cerebral mucormycosis

explanation.short: D. Rhino-orbital-cerebral mucormycosis
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Rhino-orbital-cerebral mucormycosis

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Bacterial sinusitis : Incorrect . While bacterial sinusitis can present with symptoms of facial pain and fever , it typically does not cause black eschar on the nasal mucosa or bony destruction seen on imaging.
  • • Option A. Bacterial sinusitis
  • • Incorrect
  • • present with symptoms of facial pain and fever
  • • Option B. Allergic fungal sinusitis: Incorrect . Allergic fungal sinusitis can present with similar sinus symptoms , but it is more common in individuals with a history of atopy and generally does not produce black eschar or lead to disorientation.
  • • Option B. Allergic fungal sinusitis:
  • • Incorrect
  • • present with similar sinus symptoms
  • • more common in individuals with a history of atopy
  • • not produce black eschar
  • • Option C. Nasal cellulitis : Incorrect . Nasal cellulitis may present with facial pain and fever , but it would not explain the black eschar, bony destruction, or the patient's altered mental status.
  • • Option C. Nasal cellulitis
  • • Incorrect
  • • present with facial pain and fever

subject: ENT
question: A 16-year-old boy presents with fever, headache, and swelling around his left eye. He has a history of chronic rhinosinusitis. On examination, he exhibits proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and reduced visual acuity in the affected eye. A CT scan of the sinuses shows ethmoid sinusitis and evidence of an orbital abscess. Which of the following is the most likely complication of sinusitis in this patient?
options: Superior Orbital Fissure Syndrome (SOFS)
Orbital Apex Syndrome (OAS)
Frontal Mucocele
Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis

explanation.short: B. Orbital Apex Syndrome (OAS)
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Orbital Apex Syndrome (OAS)

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Superior Orbital Fissure Syndrome (SOFS): Incorrect . SOFS involves dysfunction of the structures passing through the superior orbital fissure , including cranial nerves III, IV, V1 , and VI , leading to ophthalmoplegia and corneal anesthesia , but typically not reduced visual acuity.
  • • Option A. Superior Orbital Fissure Syndrome (SOFS):
  • • Incorrect
  • • dysfunction of the structures passing through the superior orbital fissure
  • • cranial nerves III, IV, V1
  • • VI
  • • leading to ophthalmoplegia
  • • corneal anesthesia
  • • Option C. Frontal Mucocele : Incorrect . A frontal mucocele typically presents with painless, progressive proptosis and displacement of the eye but does not usually cause acute ophthalmoplegia or reduced visual acuity unless complicated by infection or rupture.
  • • Option C. Frontal Mucocele
  • • Incorrect
  • • painless, progressive proptosis
  • • displacement of the eye
  • • Option D. Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis : Incorrect . While cavernous sinus thrombosis can lead to ophthalmoplegia and reduced visual acuity , it would also typically present with signs of cavernous sinus involvement , such as bilateral eye symptoms , cranial nerve deficits , and possibly altered mental status.
  • • Option D. Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis
  • • Incorrect
  • • lead to ophthalmoplegia
  • • reduced visual acuity
  • • present with signs of cavernous sinus involvement
  • • bilateral eye symptoms
  • • cranial nerve deficits

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Orbital Apex Syndrome is a serious complication of sinusitis , particularly ethmoid sinusitis , which can lead to a combination of ophthalmoplegia , proptosis , and reduced visual acuity due to the involvement of cranial nerves passing through the superior orbital fissure and the optic nerve .
  • ➤ Orbital Apex Syndrome
  • ➤ serious complication of sinusitis
  • ➤ ethmoid sinusitis
  • ➤ lead to a combination of ophthalmoplegia
  • ➤ proptosis
  • ➤ reduced visual acuity

subject: ENT
question: A 24-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 4-day history of painful oral ulcers. She has no fever, lymphadenopathy, or systemic symptoms. On examination, there are multiple 3–5 mm round ulcers with central yellowish necrotic areas and surrounding erythematous halos on the inner surface of her lips and buccal mucosa. The ulcers are tender to touch. She reports having similar episodes over the past few months, especially during periods of stress. She is otherwise healthy and not on any long-term medications. Which of the following features would best support the diagnosis of aphthous ulcers?
options: Sparing of hard palate and gingiva with absence of systemic symptoms
Presence of fever, cervical lymphadenopathy, and involvement of gingiva
Involvement of non-movable mucosa with single large ulcer healing without scarring
Vesicular lesions on both skin and mucosa with accompanying malaise and myalgia

explanation.short: A. Sparing of hard palate and gingiva with absence of systemic symptoms
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Sparing of hard palate and gingiva with absence of systemic symptoms

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Aphthous ulcers typically involve movable mucosa, spare the hard palate and gingiva, recur frequently, and are not associated with systemic symptoms.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 218
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 218

subject: ENT
question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric ENT clinic by his parents who are concerned that he has difficulty pronouncing certain words. On examination, there is a short, thick lingual frenulum attaching the underside of the tongue to the floor of the mouth. However, the child is able to protrude his tongue beyond the lower incisors. There is no drooling, feeding difficulty, or dental hygiene issue noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate frenotomy under local anesthesia
Reassure the parents that speech issues are unlikely due to tongue-tie if the tongue can protrude beyond lower incisors
Refer to speech therapy followed by surgical correction
Begin prophylactic antibiotics and schedule for surgical correction

explanation.short: B. Reassure the parents that speech issues are unlikely due to tongue-tie if the tongue can protrude beyond lower incisors
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Reassure the parents that speech issues are unlikely due to tongue-tie if the tongue can protrude beyond lower incisors

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Ankyloglossia rarely causes speech issues if the tongue can protrude beyond the lower incisors; reassurance is appropriate in such cases.

subject: ENT
question: A 55-year-old man presents for evaluation of a red patch on the inner aspect of his lower jaw that he noticed incidentally while brushing. He has a 30-year history of tobacco chewing. On examination, there is a velvety red, non-ulcerated patch over the gingivobuccal sulcus, not associated with pain or bleeding. A biopsy is planned. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Prescribe antifungal mouthwash and observe for 2 weeks
Perform excision biopsy due to high risk of malignant transformation
Reassure the patient, as red patches in the oral cavity are usually benign and self-limiting
Begin topical corticosteroid therapy for symptomatic relief

explanation.short: B. Perform excision biopsy due to high risk of malignant transformation
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Perform excision biopsy due to high risk of malignant transformation

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Erythroplakia is a red mucosal patch with high malignant potential and requires prompt excision biopsy for diagnosis and management.

subject: ENT
question: A 38-year-old woman presents with a painless, slowly enlarging mass in the region just below her right ear. She has no facial weakness or systemic symptoms. On examination, a firm, mobile mass is noted at the tail of the right parotid gland. Imaging confirms a well-defined lesion involving the superficial lobe of the parotid. Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) suggests a benign mixed tumour. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Observation, as pleomorphic adenomas rarely recur
Superficial parotidectomy with a margin of normal tissue
Incisional biopsy followed by simple enucleation of the mass
Chemotherapy followed by radiotherapy due to high risk of malignancy

explanation.short: B. Superficial parotidectomy with a margin of normal tissue
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Superficial parotidectomy with a margin of normal tissue

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Pleomorphic adenoma is a benign, slow-growing mixed tumour of the salivary glands best treated by superficial parotidectomy with a margin of normal tissue to prevent recurrence.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 232–233.

subject: ENT
question: A 58-year-old male with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with a progressively enlarging, non-healing ulcer on the floor of the mouth. Examination reveals a 5 cm ulcerative lesion involving the floor of the mouth and extending into the underlying cortical bone. A firm, non-tender 4.5 cm lymph node is palpable in the ipsilateral level Ib region. Imaging confirms bony invasion and a single ipsilateral lymph node without distant metastasis. What is the TNM stage and overall stage of this patient’s oral cavity carcinoma?
options: T2N2aM0 – Stage III
T4aN2aM0 – Stage IV A
T3N3M0 – Stage IV B
T4bN2cM1 – Stage IV C

explanation.short: B. T4aN2aM0 – Stage IV A
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) T4aN2aM0 – Stage IV A

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ A lesion invading adjacent structures (e.g., cortical bone) with a 3–6 cm ipsilateral node and no distant metastasis is classified as T4aN2aM0, corresponding to Stage IV A oral cavity carcinoma.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 226–227 (AJCC 2002 TNM Staging).
  • ➤ Ref: P.L Dhingra Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 226–227 (AJCC 2002 TNM Staging).

subject: ENT
question: An 82-year-old dehydrated woman with a history of recent prolonged hospitalization presents with fever, severe pain in the region near her left jaw, and difficulty moving her jaw. On examination, her left parotid gland is swollen, tender, and erythematous. Gentle pressure over the gland results in the expression of purulent discharge from the Stensen’s duct. Her WBC count is elevated with neutrophilic predominance. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient?
options: Start oral antiviral therapy and monitor for spontaneous drainage
Initiate intravenous antibiotics, ensure hydration, and encourage salivary flow
Schedule immediate surgical drainage without attempting conservative management
Begin corticosteroids to reduce gland inflammation and suppress immune response

explanation.short: B. Initiate intravenous antibiotics, ensure hydration, and encourage salivary flow
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Initiate intravenous antibiotics, ensure hydration, and encourage salivary flow

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Acute suppurative parotitis is managed with IV antibiotics, hydration, oral hygiene, and salivary stimulation; surgery is reserved for refractory cases.

subject: ENT
question: A 62-year-old male smoker presents with a painless, slowly enlarging mass below his right ear for the past few months. Examination reveals a smooth, well-defined, non-tender mass in the tail of the parotid gland. Imaging shows a cystic lesion. FNAC reveals a benign neoplasm with both epithelial and lymphoid components. The lesion is suspected to contain brownish fluid. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pleomorphic adenoma
Warthin tumor (Adenolymphoma)
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
Oncocytoma

explanation.short: B. Warthin tumor (Adenolymphoma)
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Warthin tumor (Adenolymphoma)

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

subject: ENT
question: A 12-year-old girl presents with a painless, slow-growing mass in the region of the left parotid gland. Imaging shows a well-defined lesion within the superficial lobe. FNAC reveals both mucin-producing and squamous epithelial cells. The lesion is diagnosed as a low-grade mucoepidermoid tumour. There is no facial nerve involvement on exam. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation only, as low-grade parotid tumors in children rarely recur
Superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation
Chemoradiation without surgery due to young age
Radical parotidectomy with facial nerve sacrifice and neck dissection

Ans. B) Superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation
