subject: ENT
question: A 50-year-old woman presents with a gradually enlarging, painless mass in the region of the left parotid gland. She reports recent onset of left-sided facial weakness and constant dull pain radiating to her ear. On examination, there is a firm, fixed mass involving the deep lobe of the parotid, with House-Brackmann grade III facial nerve palsy. Imaging shows perineural spread of the tumor. Biopsy reveals a diagnosis of adenoid cystic carcinoma. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation and no further treatment
Chemoradiation alone, as surgery is not effective in perineural tumors
Radical parotidectomy with wide margins and postoperative radiotherapy if margins are positive
Total parotidectomy with elective neck dissection in all patients, regardless of nodal status

Ans. C) Radical parotidectomy with wide margins and postoperative radiotherapy if margins are positive

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding the anatomy of the palatine tonsil is correct?

Lateral surface of the tonsil is covered by respiratory epithelium and is adherent to the superior constrictor
The upper pole of the tonsil is in contact with the tongue and forms the anterior tonsillar space

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following correctly matches the part of the pharynx with its anatomical extent and vertebral level?
options: Oropharynx – extends from skull base to soft palate; lies opposite C1
Laryngopharynx – extends from hyoid to lower border of cricoid; lies opposite C3–C6
Nasopharynx – extends from soft palate to hyoid bone; lies opposite C2–C3
Oropharynx – extends from nasal cavity to cricoid cartilage; lies opposite C4–C6

Mandibular nerve block to relieve referred pain
Styloid process excision via transoral or cervical approach

subject: ENT
question: A 17-year-old male presents with complaints of persistent postnasal drip, nasal obstruction, recurrent sore throat, and dull occipital headaches for the past few months. On examination of the nasopharynx, a midline cystic swelling with crusting is seen on the posterior wall. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

Thornwaldt’s disease

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding pharyngeal and adjacent anatomical structures is correct?

Thornwaldt cyst is a remnant of the second pharyngeal pouch and arises from the lateral wall of the nasopharynx

subject: ENT
question: A 25-year-old man presents with fever, severe sore throat, trismus, and painful swelling just below the angle of the mandible. What is the next best step in management?
options: Transoral drainage of the abscess under local anesthesia
Needle aspiration through the tonsillar fossa to decompress the collection
Horizontal incision 2–3 cm below the angle of mandible under general anesthesia
Interval tonsillectomy after initial conservative antibiotic therapy

question: A 15-year-old boy with a known diagnosis of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA) presents with nasal obstruction, profuse recurrent epistaxis, and left facial swelling. Endoscopic surgical excision within 24–48 hours of embolization
Plan radiotherapy to the nasopharynx to achieve long-term regression

subject: ENT
question: A 2-year-old child presents with fever, difficulty swallowing, neck stiffness, and stridor. Examination reveals a unilateral bulge in the posterior pharyngeal wall. Imaging confirms a deep neck space infection. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the anatomical location and implications of this infection?
options: It lies anterior to the vertebral bodies and extends from skull base to coccyx
It is located between buccopharyngeal fascia and prevertebral fascia, and may spread to the mediastinum
It is separated by a vertical midline raphe into symmetrical prevertebral compartments
It usually arises from tuberculosis of the cervical spine and presents with bilateral bulging of the pharynx

Pyriform sinus carcinoma; laryngectomy and partial pharyngectomy with neck dissection is indicated for localized disease

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following statements regarding deep neck spaces and fasciae is correct?
options: The danger space lies between the buccopharyngeal and prevertebral fascia and extends up to the coccyx
Retropharyngeal space lies behind the alar fascia and contains the danger space
The alar fascia separates the retropharyngeal space from the danger space, which extends inferiorly up to T4
Prevertebral space lies between the alar and buccopharyngeal fasciae and terminates at the mediastinum

explanation.short: C. The alar fascia separates the retropharyngeal space from the danger space, which extends inferiorly up to T4
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) The alar fascia separates the retropharyngeal space from the danger space, which extends inferiorly up to T4

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The alar fascia separates the retropharyngeal space from the danger space, which extends from skull base to T4 and can spread infection to the mediastinum.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6 th Edition, pg 248.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6 th Edition, pg 248.

subject: ENT
question: Which of the following structures pass through the gap between the middle and inferior constrictor muscles of the pharynx?
options: Stylopharyngeus muscle and glossopharyngeal nerve
Recurrent laryngeal nerve and inferior laryngeal vessels
Internal laryngeal nerve and superior laryngeal vessels
Eustachian tube and levator veli palatini muscle

explanation.short: C. Internal laryngeal nerve and superior laryngeal vessels
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Internal laryngeal nerve and superior laryngeal vessels

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The internal laryngeal nerve and superior laryngeal vessels pass through the gap between the middle and inferior constrictor muscles of the pharynx.

subject: ENT
question: A 19-year-old male presents with mild dysphagia, posterior throat discomfort, and a gradually enlarging midline swelling on the posterior pharyngeal wall. He also has multiple cervical lymph nodes and a history of weight loss and low-grade fever. Cervical spine X-ray reveals destruction of C3 and C4 vertebral bodies. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: IV antibiotics and emergency incision and drainage
Incision and drainage followed by full antitubercular therapy
Oral steroids and observation
Immediate tracheostomy to prevent airway obstruction

explanation.short: B. Incision and drainage followed by full antitubercular therapy
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Incision and drainage followed by full antitubercular therapy

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Chronic retropharyngeal abscess due to cervical spine tuberculosis is treated with incision and drainage followed by a full course of antitubercular therapy.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 267
  • ➤ Ref:

subject: ENT
question: A 30-year-old man presents with a foreign body sensation in the throat and halitosis. On examination, a gritty mass is felt on probing the tonsil. There is no fever, dysphagia, or systemic symptoms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Tonsillar cyst
Tonsillolith
Intratonsillar abscess
Peritonsillar abscess

explanation.short: B. Tonsillolith
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Tonsillolith

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Tonsilloliths present with halitosis and a foreign body sensation and are diagnosed by a gritty feel on probing the tonsil.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 261

subject: ENT
question: A 7-year-old child presents with low-grade fever and a greyish-white membrane over the tonsils and soft palate. The membrane is adherent and bleeds on attempted removal. There is mild sore throat, and urine analysis reveals albuminuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute membranous tonsillitis
Diphtheria
Vincent angina
Infectious mononucleosis

explanation.short: B. Diphtheria
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Diphtheria

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Diphtheria presents with an adherent, dirty grey membrane over the tonsils and soft palate that bleeds on removal and is confirmed by detection of Corynebacterium diphtheriae.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 260

subject: ENT
question: A 50-year-old male presents with nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and diplopia. Imaging reveals a mass in the nasopharynx with extension into the orbit and skull base. Clinical examination shows bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, each node less than 6 cm, with no distant metastasis. What is the correct clinical stage of this patient’s nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
options: Stage III
Stage IVA
Stage IVB
Stage IVC

explanation.short: B. Stage IVA
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Stage IVA

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ T4N2M0 nasopharyngeal carcinoma is classified as Stage IVA due to intracranial and orbital involvement with bilateral lymph node metastasis and no distant spread.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 253

subject: ENT
question: A 35-year-old man presents with progressive left-sided hearing loss and a painless neck lump just below the angle of the mandible. He also reports intermittent nasal obstruction and occasional epistaxis. Otoscopy reveals a dull, retracted tympanic membrane. Neurological examination reveals decreased corneal reflex on the left and mild weakness of left lateral gaze. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Chronic suppurative otitis media with cholesteatoma
Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
Glomus jugulare tumor

explanation.short: C. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Nasopharyngeal carcinoma

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Nasopharyngeal carcinoma commonly presents with cervical lymphadenopathy, unilateral serous otitis media, nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and cranial nerve palsies due to skull base extension.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 251
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 251

subject: ENT
question: A 34-year-old woman presents with progressive dysphagia, weight loss, and a history of iron-deficiency anemia. She has no significant smoking or alcohol history. Indirect laryngoscopy does not reveal any mass, but pooling of secretions is noted in the hypopharynx. Barium swallow reveals an irregular narrowing in the postcricoid region. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis, and what is an important associated risk factor?
options: Carcinoma of the upper esophagus; associated with Barrett's esophagus
Carcinoma of the pyriform sinus; associated with heavy smoking and alcohol use
Carcinoma of the postcricoid region; associated with Plummer–Vinson syndrome
Carcinoma of the larynx; associated with vocal abuse and laryngopharyngeal reflux

explanation.short: C. Carcinoma of the postcricoid region; associated with Plummer–Vinson syndrome
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Carcinoma of the postcricoid region; associated with Plummer–Vinson syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Carcinoma of the postcricoid region is strongly associated with Plummer–Vinson syndrome and presents with progressive dysphagia, especially in young females.
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 274
  • ➤ Ref: P.L. Dhingra, Diseases of Ear, Nose and Throat and Head and Neck Surgery, 6th Edition, pg 274
